sv_articles.title: Activity: Intro: Coming Together, Falling Apart
sv_articles.text:
The student council at your school starts the year with a clear purpose: making the school better for everyone. Representatives from each grade sit in a circle, share ideas, and take careful notes. They agree to fix the worst problems first, regardless of which hallway or classroom they are representing. Early meetings feel hopeful. One week, the council members vote to obtain more library books; another week, they approve painting the most worn-out lockers. When the council members disagree, they listen to each other and adjust their plans. Students begin to trust that the council is a fair, united group.

As the year goes on, however, one representative from the oldest grade begins to change their attitude. They argue that since the students in their grade are doing the most fundraising and are the oldest, their classrooms deserve the biggest projects. They take the seat at the head of the table, collect all the suggestion forms, and start to decide which ideas will even get voted on. When committees are formed, they put themselves in charge and choose mostly students from their own grade to help. Slowly, more of the budget and attention flows toward their classrooms. Representatives from younger grades notice that their requests are delayed or ignored. They begin to feel pushed aside and mistrustful. The council that once felt like everyone was working together in one shared effort now feels like a struggle between competing sides.

sv_articles.title: Activity 1: The Persian Wars
sv_articles.text:
The Persian Empire

The Persian Empire existed for more than 200 years. It lasted from around 550 to 330 B.C.E. The empire stretched geographically from Greece to India. The Persians came from a region in southwest Asia that is now called Iran. During this time, this region was called Parsa. The Persians were skilled warriors. They had powerful rulers. Though short-lived compared to some, their empire became one of the largest and most powerful in ancient history.

Persian kings such as Cyrus the Great and Darius I conquered older civilizations, including Mesopotamia and Egypt. These kings claimed lands along the eastern Mediterranean coast. They ruled this huge area by dividing it into provinces. They put governors in charge. The governors collected taxes and kept order for the king. A network of roads connected the cities of the empire. These roads helped soldiers, messengers, and traders move more quickly across long distances. People in the empire spoke many different languages and followed different traditions. However, they were all subjects of the Persian king. For nearly two centuries, Persia was the strongest empire in this part of the world.

Problems with Persia

While the Persian Empire grew and expanded in the east, the powerful city-states of Athens and Sparta emerged as dominant forces on mainland Greece. Before the Persians tried to conquer mainland Greece, they had already taken control of many Greek city-states along the coast of Asia Minor, in a region called Ionia. Many people in these Ionian cities disliked being ruled by leaders who were loyal to Persia, so they rebelled. Some mainland Greek city-states, including Athens, helped the rebels. The revolt was crushed, but the Persian king was angry. He wanted to punish the Greeks who had supported the rebellion. Around 492 B.C.E., the Persian Empire threatened to conquer Greece. This worried both Athenian and Spartan leaders. Both city-states knew a united front was vital against this common enemy.

Athens had a strong navy. Sparta had a well-trained army. These city-states formed an alliance to repel the Persians. Through a series of battles, the Greeks held off the Persian army. By the end of the Persian Wars, the Greeks had stopped the Persian invasions and kept their independence.

The Battle of Marathon (490 B.C.E.)

On the plains of Marathon, Athenian citizen soldiers, or hoplites, faced a large Persian army. Hoplites were heavily armed with shields and spears. Despite being outnumbered, the Athenians used military strategies and a surprise charge to defeat the Persian forces. The Persian army was stunned that they could be defeated by the Athenians. This defeat sent a shock wave through the Persian empire. The victory brought Greek soldiers together and made them feel more confident in their ability to win the war when the city-states were unified.

The Battle of Salamis (480 B.C.E.)

The Greek forces had a navy with experienced crews of sailors. The Greek navy was superior to the Persian naval forces. Knowing this, the Greeks strategically lured the Persian navy into the straits of Salamis. A strait is a narrow body of water that connects two larger bodies of water. Because of their size, Persian ships could not easily turn around in the straits. This opened up the Persian ships to attack. Greek warships rammed and sank many Persian vessels. This naval battle halted the Persian invasion of Greece. After losing so many ships, the Persian king could no longer easily supply his large army in Greece, so many Persian forces withdrew.

The Battle of Plataea (479 B.C.E.)

After the Greek naval victory at Salamis, the Persian king Xerxes returned to Asia. He left a large army to finish the conquest of Greece. A united Greek force, led on land by Sparta and joined by Athens and many other city-states, marched to face this army near the town of Plataea. Many of Persia's soldiers were conscripts, or people who had been forced to fight against their wishes. When the fighting began, the heavily armored Greek hoplites broke through the Persian front line. Most of the Persian army, including the general, Mardonius, were captured or killed. At the same time, Greek naval forces launched an attack on Persian ships on the coast. These coordinated attacks destroyed Persia's army and navy. This ended any attempt to invade Greece again.

The Persian Wars had a lasting impact on Greek society. Fighting side by side helped many Greeks see themselves as part of one larger people, not just citizens of separate cities. The soldiers also saw how this unity strengthened them when they worked together. After the Persian Wars, Athens's leadership of the navy gave it greater influence among the Greek city-states. Athens started a new alliance, and many members sent ships or money that helped Athens build a stronger navy and impressive public buildings. This rising power made Sparta and its allies afraid that Athens would dominate Greece. This rivalry threatened the unity that Greek city-states had fought so hard to build.

Fun Fact: The Persian Empire covered 3.5 million square miles. That's as large as Mexico and India put together. The United States of America covers approximately 3.8 million square miles of Earth.

Holding the Hot Gates

Thermopylae was a narrow mountain pass in northern Greece. The name “Thermopylae” means “hot gates.” It earned this name because of the warm springs that flowed nearby. On one side of the pass rose steep, rocky slopes. On the other side lay the sea. The space between them was so tight that only a small number of soldiers could stand side by side.

During the Persian Wars, a small Greek force, including Spartan soldiers led by King Leonidas, chose Thermopylae as the place to block a much larger Persian army. The Persians could not bring all their troops to the front at once, so Greek soldiers wielding shields and spears held them back. Eventually, a local guide showed the Persians a hidden path through the hills, allowing them to surround the defenders. The Greeks at Thermopylae were defeated, but their stand at the “hot gates” bought time for other city-states to prepare. This event became one of the most famous stories of bravery in Greek history.

The First Marathon Runner

Our modern marathons trace their history back to the legendary actions of a Greek messenger. A few versions of the legend exist, and historians are unsure which is most accurate. In the stories, a runner had to race from the battlefield to Athens to deliver a critical message about the Battle of Marathon. He may have been asking for reinforcements or declaring victory. The messenger ran 26 miles and 385 yards through the hills of Greece to reach Athens. In each of the versions, after arriving in Athens and delivering the message, the runner collapsed from exhaustion.

Centuries later, people in the modern world were inspired by these stories. When the modern Olympic Games began in 1896, organizers created a long-distance race to honor the legendary run from Marathon to Athens. Today, people around the world compete in races called marathons that are exactly 26.2 miles long.

sv_articles.title: Activity 2: The Political Leagues
sv_articles.text:
Highlight or underline the reasons that the Delian League and Peloponnesian League were established.

The Delian League

After the Persian Wars, many of the Greek city-states realized the need for a unified defense against future threats. In 478 B.C.E., the city-states formed the Delian League. The league was a military agreement led by Athens. The main goal was to protect Greek cities from any future Persian attack, and the members agreed to share their resources. At first, each member city-state either sent warships and soldiers or paid money into the league's treasury to keep the alliance strong. They did this every year. The league's treasury was initially located on the island of Delos. The Delian League contributed to a period of cultural prosperity. This was known as the Golden Age of Athens.

The Delian League successfully freed Greek cities in Asia Minor and the Aegean Sea from Persian control. However, Athens began to exercise increasing control over the league. Some city-states began to question why they still had to send so much money to the treasury. Then the treasury was moved to Athens. The money began to be spent on monuments and temples in Athens. Other city-states in Greece grew resentful.

The island city-state of Thasos tried to leave the Delian League. Athens sent its navy to attack the city. The navy soldiers tore down Thasos's walls. They took control of its ships and nearby mines. They forced Thasos to rejoin the league and keep paying money. Because Athens seemed to be ruling the other members instead of treating them as equal partners, many Greeks began to say that the Delian League had turned into an Athenian empire.

The Peloponnesian League

Sparta and Corinth felt threatened by the Delian League's control over the region. They were not part of the Delian League. They saw it as a threat to their independence. To balance the power, Sparta began reorganizing its alliances with other city-states, including Corinth. The Peloponnesian League began to offer protection to the city-states that did not want to be controlled by Athens, to entice them to ally with Sparta. Sparta and its allies claimed that their league would protect the independence of Greek city-states that did not want to be controlled by Athens. Many city-states that were angry about Athenian power or feared losing their freedom chose to side with Sparta.

As more city-states chose sides between Athens and Sparta, the Greek world split into two rival camps. This deep division contributed to the Peloponnesian War.

Primary Source Activity: Perspectives from the Past

Thucydides was an Athenian general and historian who lived during the Peloponnesian War. After being exiled from Athens, he wrote a detailed history of the conflict between Athens and Sparta. In his book, he includes speeches that he says are based on what leaders actually said or what best expressed their ideas. These speeches help us understand how Athenians and their allies explained their empire and how Spartans and their allies explained their fears.

The quotes below show different perspectives from each side. Read each quote and work in pairs to discuss its meaning. Then, write about the perspectives each league held.

The Athenian/Delian League Perspective         The Spartan/Peloponnesian League Perspective

That empire we acquired by no violent means, but because you were unwilling to prosecute to its conclusion the war against the barbarian … fear being our principal motive, though honour and interest afterwards came in.

—Athenian envoys speaking to the Spartans

Your empire is a despotism and your subjects disaffected conspirators, whose obedience is ensured not by your suicidal concessions, but by the superiority given you.

—Spartan addressing the Athenian Assembly

We did accept an empire that was offered to us, and refused to give it up under the pressure of fear, honour, and interest.

—Athenian envoys speaking to the Spartans

We meanwhile are the same then and now, and shall not, if we are wise, disregard the wrongs of our allies.… Others have much money and ships and horses, but we have good allies whom we must not give up to the Athenians.

—Spartan leader Sthenelaidas, urging the Spartan Assembly to declare war

It has always been law that the weaker should be subject to the stronger.

—Athenian envoys speaking to the Spartans

The quality which they condemn is really nothing but a wise moderation…. We are both warlike and wise, and it is our sense of order that makes us so.

—King Archidamus of Sparta, addressing the Spartan Assembly

sv_articles.title: Article 3: The Peloponnesian War
sv_articles.text:
Highlight and underline the factors that led to the end of the Peloponnesian War.

The rivalry between the Peloponnesian League and the Delian League led to a long and disastrous conflict known as the Peloponnesian War. Sparta was the head of the Peloponnesian League. The Delian League was led by Athens. The war occurred in two stages. The first stage lasted from 460 to 446 B.C.E. The second stage lasted from 431 to 404 B.C.E. It included many of the Greek city-states.

For nearly 50 years, these former allies fought a brutal war. The conflict divided the region of Greece. At first, the Athenian navy dominated in battles at sea. The Athenians controlled trade routes and limited supply lines. Later in the war, Sparta received money from the Persian Empire to build a stronger navy. With this help, the Spartans were able to challenge Athens at sea. They began to win important battles. The Spartan fleet destroyed most of the Athenian ships. Without its navy, Athens could not bring in enough food or supplies and was forced to surrender.

Sparta’s strong army also eventually wore down the Athenians. Sparta’s allies and battle strategies helped them win key land battles. This cut off Athenian supplies and weakened their grip on power. As the war dragged on, thousands of people crowded inside Athens’s city walls to stay safe from Spartan attacks, and a deadly plague swept through the city, killing many people, including Athens's leader, Pericles. Sparta emerged victorious.

The Peloponnesian War left the city-states in Greece weakened and fractured. Athens’s former power and influence were shattered. The city’s walls were torn down, most of its warships were taken away, and Athens had to give up control of its empire. For a short time, a small group of pro-Spartan leaders called the 30 Tyrants ruled Athens harshly, punished their opponents, and caused fear and unrest in the city.

After the Peloponnesian War, Sparta tried to use its victory to control other Greek city-states. Spartan leaders placed friendly governments in many cities and interfered in their politics. Instead of creating peace, this angered other Greeks and led to even more wars and rebellions. Over time, both Athens and Sparta lost strength as they fought against new enemies. The city-states of Greece became poorer, more divided, and less able to work together. The wars left them weakened and vulnerable at a time when other empires were beginning to grow.

sv_articles.title: Activity 4: Primary Source Analysis: Pericles’s Funeral Oration
sv_articles.text:
In 431 B.C.E., Pericles gave a funeral oration, or a formal speech, for all of the soldiers who had died in the Peloponnesian War. His speech focused on Athenian democracy and achievements. Historians believe the speech was recorded and rewritten by Thucydides, a Greek historian. Since this speech was written in ancient Greek, there are many different translations. This text is an excerpt from a translation of Pericles’s speech.

I shall begin with our ancestors: it is both just and proper that they should have the honour of the first mention on an occasion like the present. They dwelt in the country without break in the succession from generation to generation, and handed it down free to the present time by their valour. And if our more remote ancestors deserve praise, much more do our own fathers, who added to their inheritance the empire which we now possess, and spared no pains to be able to leave their acquisitions to us of the present generation. Lastly, there are few parts of our dominions that have not been augmented by those of us here, who are still more or less in the vigour of life; while the mother country has been furnished by us with everything that can enable her to depend on her own resources whether for war or for peace. That part of our history which tells of the military achievements which gave us our several possessions, or of the ready valour with which either we or our fathers stemmed the tide of Hellenic or foreign aggression, is a theme too familiar to my hearers for me to dilate on, and I shall therefore pass it by. But what was the road by which we reached our position, what the form of government under which our greatness grew, what the national habits out of which it sprang; these are questions which I may try to solve before I proceed to my panegyric upon these men; since I think this to be a subject upon which on the present occasion a speaker may properly dwell, and to which the whole assemblage, whether citizens or foreigners, may listen with advantage.

Our constitution does not copy the laws of neighbouring states; we are rather a pattern to others than imitators ourselves. Its administration favours the many instead of the few; this is why it is called a democracy. If we look to the laws, they afford equal justice to all in their private differences; if no social standing, advancement in public life falls to reputation for capacity, class considerations not being allowed to interfere with merit; nor again does poverty bar the way, if a man is able to serve the state, he is not hindered by the obscurity of his condition. The freedom which we enjoy in our government extends also to our ordinary life. There, far from exercising a jealous surveillance over each other, we do not feel called upon to be angry with our neighbour for doing what he likes, or even to indulge in those injurious looks which cannot fail to be offensive, although they inflict no positive penalty. But all this ease in our private relations does not make us lawless as citizens. Against this fear is our chief safeguard, teaching us to obey the magistrates and the laws, particularly such as regard the protection of the injured, whether they are actually on the statute book, or belong to that code which, although unwritten, yet cannot be broken without acknowledged disgrace.

sv_articles.title: Article 6: Weekly Connection
sv_articles.text:
The Persian Empire grew very large, from Greece all the way to India. When the Persians tried to conquer Greece, Athens and Sparta joined forces to fight them. The Greeks won important battles, including the battles of Marathon and Salamis, showing that they were stronger when united.

After the Persian Wars, Athens led the Delian League, an alliance to keep Greece safe. Athens started using the league for its own power and money. This upset other city-states. Sparta and its allies created the Peloponnesian League to challenge Athens. The rivalry between Athens and Sparta led to the Peloponnesian War. Athens was strong on the sea, but Sparta was strong on land. In the end, Sparta won, but both sides were left weaker. This fighting made the Greek city-states less powerful.

Pericles’s funeral oration reminded Athenians of their democracy and achievements, even while they were at war. His speech showed what Athenians valued the most.

sections.name: Teacher Background Knowledge
sections.content:
Ancient Athens is known for its invention of democracy, which emerged after reformers challenged the rule of wealthy nobles. Cleisthenes established a council and an Assembly, giving ordinary citizens a voice. Athenian democracy flourished under Pericles, who furthered citizen participation by introducing pay for public service. Despite Athens's achievements during the golden age, Plato criticized democracy, advocating for philosopher-kings. Nevertheless, Athenian democracy's influence endures, with its principles inspiring modern democratic systems worldwide.
The Greco-Persian Wars were fought from about 492 B.C.E. to 449 B.C.E. between the small city-states of Greece and the First Persian Empire, which was one of the largest empires of all time. The wars ended with the Greeks maintaining their independence from Persia. Greeks remember the two most famous battles of the Persian Wars as evidence of their people's great courage and resourcefulness. At the Battle of Marathon in 490 B.C.E., an army from the city of Athens expelled the Persians from the Greek mainland. At the Battle of Salamis in 480 B.C.E., the superior Greek navy lured Persian ships into a strait, trapping them and ultimately destroying the Persian navy. This battle ended any hope of Persian forces taking mainland Greece.
From 460 to 446 B.C.E. and then 431 to 404 B.C.E., Athens and Sparta fought against each other in what is known as the Peloponnesian Wars. These two rival city-states fought for dominance of the southern part of the Greek peninsula, which is known as the Peloponnese. Sparta won the war, but the long conflict weakened both cities. Other parts of Greece soon came to dominate the region. First Thebes and then Macedon rose to power.

sections.name: Learning Objectives
sections.content:
Students will be able to analyze the causes and consequences of the Persian and Peloponnesian Wars.
Students will be able to explain how Athens and Sparta sometimes cooperated to defend Greece and other times fought each other for power.
Students will be able to evaluate how these wars shaped the strength and eventual decline of the Greek city-states.

This week contains a walkthrough slide deck that supports student learning.

sections.name: Unit Summary
sections.content:
Ancient Greece began on a small, mountainous peninsula that stretched into the Mediterranean Sea between Europe and Asia. Steep mountains, scattered valleys, and many islands made travel difficult and kept communities apart. Villages and towns developed independently and grew into city-states, or poleis, each with its own government, laws, and identity. People thought of themselves first as citizens of places such as Athens, Sparta, or Thebes, not simply as "Greek." The sea was central to life. Limited good farmland pushed many people toward shipbuilding, sailing, fishing, and trade, so harbors and coastlines became busy centers of exchange.
Over time, different city-states experienced different types of government. Some were monarchies ruled by kings. Others became oligarchies where a small group of wealthy men shared power. At times, individuals seized control as tyrants. In Athens, reformers eventually pushed the city toward democracy. Cleisthenes reorganized Athenian society in 508 B.C.E., creating a council of 500 citizens chosen by lottery and an assembly where citizens debated and voted on laws. Later, Pericles strengthened this system by paying jurors and officials so poorer citizens could serve. Under his leadership, Athens entered a golden age of art, architecture, and philosophy. At the same time, thinkers such as Plato criticized democracy and argued that only wise, trained philosopher-kings should rule. Even with its limits and critics, Athenian democracy introduced the lasting idea that citizens should have a voice in how they are governed.
Religion shaped everyday life and public spaces. The Greeks practiced a polytheistic religion that honored many gods and goddesses who looked and acted like humans. Zeus ruled the sky with thunderbolts, Poseidon ruled the sea, and Athena represented wisdom and war. These and other deities were believed to live on Mount Olympus, yet they often stepped into human affairs. Myths told stories of the gods falling in love, arguing, taking sides in wars, and seeking revenge, which helped people understand the divine through familiar emotions. Families kept small household altars where they offered food, wine, and incense to the gods. Cities built great temples and sanctuaries, such as the Acropolis in Athens and the sanctuary of Apollo at Delphi. Festivals with processions, music, athletics, and drama, including the Panathenaia and the Olympic Games, brought communities together and reinforced a shared Greek identity. Greek religion had no single holy book; instead, myths and rituals taught moral lessons and linked worship to daily life.
Ideas also spread through philosophy and art. Socrates encouraged people to question their beliefs. Plato wrote about justice, government, and truth, and founded the Academy in Athens. Aristotle focused on observation and logic, studied science and politics, and founded the Lyceum. Their work influenced education, civic life, and ways of thinking about knowledge. Greek architecture, especially the use of columns and carefully planned temples, expressed values of order, balance, and beauty. Buildings on the Acropolis, including the Parthenon, showed both religious devotion and civic pride.
Greek city-states sometimes united against outside threats. The Persian Empire grew into one of the largest empires in history and controlled Greek cities in Ionia. When these cities rebelled and Athens helped them, the Persian kings tried to punish mainland Greece. Athens and Sparta formed an alliance, combining Athenian naval strength with Spartan land forces. In battles such as Marathon, Salamis, and Plataea, Greek strategies and the use of local geography helped smaller Greek forces defeat larger Persian armies. These victories protected Greek independence and encouraged many people to see themselves as part of a larger Greek world. After the wars, Athens led a naval alliance called the Delian League. Member city-states sent ships or money to a shared treasury, which helped Athens grow richer and more powerful and contributed to its golden age.
As Persian threats faded, fear and rivalry grew within Greece. Athens began to treat Delian League members less like partners and more like subjects, forcing cities such as Thasos to remain in the alliance and continue paying tribute. Sparta and allies such as Corinth formed the Peloponnesian League, claiming to defend the independence of city-states that did not want Athenian control. The Greek world split into two hostile camps, and this deep division led to the Peloponnesian War. The conflict unfolded in two long stages and drew in almost all the major city-states. Athens at first controlled the seas, but overcrowding inside its walls and a deadly plague weakened the city. Sparta later received Persian money to build a stronger navy, destroyed much of the Athenian fleet, and forced Athens to surrender. For a time, harsh pro-Spartan rulers known as the Thirty Tyrants took power in Athens. After years of fighting, many Greek city-states were left weakened and divided.
This weakness opened the way for a northern kingdom, Macedon, to dominate Greece. King Philip of Macedon built a new kind of army that trained farmers and commoners to fight in a tight phalanx formation with long spears, supported by a strong cavalry. At the Battle of Chaeronea in 338 B.C.E., Philip and his son Alexander defeated a united force from Athens and Thebes and drew most Greek city-states into a league led by Macedon. Shortly afterward, Philip was assassinated, and Alexander became king.
Alexander set out to conquer the Persian Empire and succeeded, then went on to conquer Egypt and push his armies as far as India. His troops finally refused to march farther, and he turned back, but Greek influence had already spread across a vast region. Wherever Alexander went, his armies and settlers carried Greek language, stories, art, and building styles. Local rulers and communities began to adopt Greek architecture, build theaters and marketplaces, and use Greek in government and trade. Alexander also showed interest in the cultures he encountered, studying astronomy in Mesopotamia, exploring the Nile, and collecting objects to display in museums. At the same time, he could be brutally harsh, as when he destroyed Thebes after a rebellion.
Alexander died at age 32 without a clear heir, and his generals struggled for control. After long conflicts known as the Successor Wars, his empire broke into several Hellenistic kingdoms ruled by leaders, such as Antigonus, Seleucus, and Ptolemy. They governed lands that stretched from Macedonia and the Greek city-states to Egypt, Syria, Mesopotamia, Persia, and Central Asia. Greek became the language of many royal courts and official documents. New or expanded cities such as Alexandria, Antioch, Pergamon, and Ai Khanum combined Greek plans and buildings with local traditions. The Museum of Alexandria and the Library of Alexandria gathered texts from many cultures and became centers of study. Inscriptions such as the Rosetta Stone, written in Egyptian and Greek, and translations of older writings into Greek show how widely Hellenistic culture reached.
Over time, the Hellenistic kingdoms weakened and eventually came under Roman control. Greek influence did not disappear. Instead, Roman language, art, architecture, and political ideas absorbed and reshaped Greek models.

sections.name: Let’s Write
sections.content:
In what important ways were Athens and Sparta different from each other, and how did those differences shape the history of Greece?
How did the ideas of philosophers such as Socrates, Plato, and Aristotle challenge people in ancient Greece to think differently about government?
How did the Persian Wars and the Peloponnesian War each change the balance of power among Greek city-states and affect the future of Greece?

sections.name: Apply What You Learned
sections.content:
Choose one of the activities to show what you've learned:
WRITE:
Which city-state in ancient Greece do you think you would have wanted to live in, such as Athens, Sparta, or another polis? Write one or two paragraphs explaining your choice and the reasons for your choice, using facts you have learned.
Was Alexander the Great truly "great"? Write 1-2 paragraphs explaining your opinion using examples from his conquests and how he treated the places he ruled.
Which physical feature of Greece, such as mountains, islands, or seas, do you think had the biggest effect on how people lived? Write 1-2 paragraphs explaining your choice, and use specific examples to show how that feature affected travel, trade, or farming.
RESEARCH/PRESENT:
Choose one Greek god, goddess, or hero you would like to learn more about. Create a poster or a presentation explaining what Greek people believed about this deity and how the people worshipped the deity.
Research a famous Greek building, such as the Parthenon, or a temple from another city-state. Find out what it was used for and what its main features were. Create a labeled drawing or model and present at least three facts about the building.
SPEAK:
Work in pairs to discuss what you think was the most important idea the Greeks contributed to the world (for example, democracy, drama, philosophy, or architecture). Explain your choice, and listen to what your partner thinks. Use reasoning and examples to defend your view.
Choose a Greek myth you have learned about. Work in pairs and retell the myth in your own words, making sure to explain what lesson or message the myth teaches. Then listen to your partner's myth and ask questions about it.
Write and deliver a speech from the perspective of Pericles, urging the citizens of Athens to take pride in their democratic government. Your speech should accurately describe the features and rules of Athenian democracy.
PLAN:
Work in pairs to design a mini Greek festival for your class. Plan activities taken from ancient Greek culture, including at least one activity involving food, one involving games or sports, and one involving arts or theater. Make a list of what you would need and how you would decorate.
Work in a small group to plan a short play based on a Greek myth or historical event, such as the Battle of Marathon or life in Athens. Decide on characters, scenes, and simple props. Write a brief outline of what each person will do.
Work with a partner to design a student government based loosely on Athenian democracy. Decide who can vote, how leaders are chosen, and how decisions will be made. Write down your rules and be ready to explain why you chose them.
DRAW:
Draw a map of ancient Greece showing the surrounding seas, mountains, and at least three important city-states. Label each part and add a key. On another piece of paper, write a paragraph explaining how geography might affect those city-states
Draw a scene from daily life in either Athens or Sparta. Include buildings, clothing, and activities that match what you have learned. Be ready to explain what each part of your picture shows.
PODCAST: Choose a podcast from this unit. Listen to the podcast individually or in pairs.
Draw a picture to represent what you learned.
Write a list of things you learned.
Create 3-5 questions from the podcast.
Create a graphic organizer from the podcast content.

sections.name: Teacher Background Knowledge
sections.content:
Ancient Athens is known for its invention of democracy, which emerged after reformers challenged the rule of wealthy nobles. Cleisthenes established a council and an Assembly, giving ordinary citizens a voice. Athenian democracy flourished under Pericles, who furthered citizen participation by introducing pay for public service. Despite Athens's achievements during the golden age, Plato criticized democracy, advocating for philosopher-kings. Nevertheless, Athenian democracy's influence endures, with its principles inspiring modern democratic systems worldwide.
The Greco-Persian Wars were fought from about 492 B.C.E. to 449 B.C.E. between the small city-states of Greece and the First Persian Empire, which was one of the largest empires of all time. The wars ended with the Greeks maintaining their independence from Persia. Greeks remember the two most famous battles of the Persian Wars as evidence of their people's great courage and resourcefulness. At the Battle of Marathon in 490 B.C.E., an army from the city of Athens expelled the Persians from the Greek mainland. At the Battle of Salamis in 480 B.C.E., the superior Greek navy lured Persian ships into a strait, trapping them and ultimately destroying the Persian navy. This battle ended any hope of Persian forces taking mainland Greece.
From 460 to 446 B.C.E. and then 431 to 404 B.C.E., Athens and Sparta fought against each other in what is known as the Peloponnesian Wars. These two rival city-states fought for dominance of the southern part of the Greek peninsula, which is known as the Peloponnese. Sparta won the war, but the long conflict weakened both cities. Other parts of Greece soon came to dominate the region. First Thebes and then Macedon rose to power.

Students will be able to analyze the causes and consequences of the Persian and Peloponnesian Wars.
Students will be able to explain how Athens and Sparta sometimes cooperated to defend Greece and other times fought each other for power.
Students will be able to evaluate how these wars shaped the strength and eventual decline of the Greek city-states.

Wherever Alexander went, his armies and settlers carried Greek language, stories, art, and building styles. Local rulers and communities began to adopt Greek architecture, build theaters and marketplaces, and use Greek in government and trade. Alexander also showed interest in the cultures he encountered, studying astronomy in Mesopotamia, exploring the Nile, and collecting objects to display in museums. At the same time, he could be brutally harsh, as when he destroyed Thebes after a rebellion.
Alexander died at age 32 without a clear heir, and his generals struggled for control. After long conflicts known as the Successor Wars, his empire broke into several Hellenistic kingdoms ruled by leaders, such as Antigonus, Seleucus, and Ptolemy. They governed lands that stretched from Macedonia and the Greek city-states to Egypt, Syria, Mesopotamia, Persia, and Central Asia. Greek became the language of many royal courts and official documents. New or expanded cities such as Alexandria, Antioch, Pergamon, and Ai Khanum combined Greek plans and buildings with local traditions. The Museum of Alexandria and the Library of Alexandria gathered texts from many cultures and became centers of study. Inscriptions such as the Rosetta Stone, written in Egyptian and Greek, and translations of older writings into Greek show how widely Hellenistic culture reached.
Over time, the Hellenistic kingdoms weakened and eventually came under Roman control. Greek influence did not disappear. Instead, Roman language, art, architecture, and political ideas absorbed and reshaped Greek models.

sections.name: Teacher Background Knowledge
sections.content:
Ancient Athens is known for its invention of democracy, which emerged after reformers challenged the rule of wealthy nobles. Cleisthenes established a council and an Assembly, giving ordinary citizens a voice. Athenian democracy flourished under Pericles, who furthered citizen participation by introducing pay for public service. Despite Athens's achievements during the golden age, Plato criticized democracy, advocating for philosopher-kings. Nevertheless, Athenian democracy's influence endures, with its principles inspiring modern democratic systems worldwide.
The Greco-Persian Wars were fought from about 492 B.C.E. to 449 B.C.E. between the small city-states of Greece and the First Persian Empire, which was one of the largest empires of all time. The wars ended with the Greeks maintaining their independence from Persia. Greeks remember the two most famous battles of the Persian Wars as evidence of their people's great courage and resourcefulness. At the Battle of Marathon in 490 B.C.E., an army from the city of Athens expelled the Persians from the Greek mainland. At the Battle of Salamis in 480 B.C.E., the superior Greek navy lured Persian ships into a strait, trapping them and ultimately destroying the Persian navy. This battle ended any hope of Persian forces taking mainland Greece.
From 460 to 446 B.C.E. and then 431 to 404 B.C.E., Athens and Sparta fought against each other in what is known as the Peloponnesian Wars. These two rival city-states fought for dominance of the southern part of the Greek peninsula, which is known as the Peloponnese. Sparta won the war, but the long conflict weakened both cities. Other parts of Greece soon came to dominate the region. First Thebes and then Macedon rose to power.

sections.name: Unit Summary
sections.content:
Ancient Greece began on a small, mountainous peninsula that stretched into the Mediterranean Sea between Europe and Asia. Steep mountains, scattered valleys, and many islands made travel difficult and kept communities apart. Villages and towns developed independently and grew into city-states, or poleis, each with its own government, laws, and identity. People thought of themselves first as citizens of places such as Athens, Sparta, or Thebes, not simply as "Greek." The sea was central to life. Limited good farmland pushed many people toward shipbuilding, sailing, fishing, and trade, so harbors and coastlines became busy centers of exchange.
Over time, different city-states experienced different types of government. Some were monarchies ruled by kings. Others became oligarchies where a small group of wealthy men shared power. At times, individuals seized control as tyrants. In Athens, reformers eventually pushed the city toward democracy. Cleisthenes reorganized Athenian society in 508 B.C.E., creating a council of 500 citizens chosen by lottery and an assembly where citizens debated and voted on laws. Later, Pericles strengthened this system by paying jurors and officials so poorer citizens could serve. Under his leadership, Athens entered a golden age of art, architecture, and philosophy. At the same time, thinkers such as Plato criticized democracy and argued that only wise, trained philosopher-kings should rule. Even with its limits and critics, Athenian democracy introduced the lasting idea that citizens should have a voice in how they are governed.
Religion shaped everyday life and public spaces. The Greeks practiced a polytheistic religion that honored many gods and goddesses who looked and acted like humans. Zeus ruled the sky with thunderbolts, Poseidon ruled the sea, and Athena represented wisdom and war. These and other deities were believed to live on Mount Olympus, yet they often stepped into human affairs. Myths told stories of the gods falling in love, arguing, taking sides in wars, and seeking revenge, which helped people understand the divine through familiar emotions. Families kept small household altars where they offered food, wine, and incense to the gods. Cities built great temples and sanctuaries, such as the Acropolis in Athens and the sanctuary of Apollo at Delphi. Festivals with processions, music, athletics, and drama, including the Panathenaia and the Olympic Games, brought communities together and reinforced a shared Greek identity. Greek religion had no single holy book; instead, myths and rituals taught moral lessons and linked worship to daily life.
Ideas also spread through philosophy and art. Socrates encouraged people to question their beliefs. Plato wrote about justice, government, and truth, and founded the Academy in Athens. Aristotle focused on observation and logic, studied science and politics, and founded the Lyceum. Their work influenced education, civic life, and ways of thinking about knowledge. Greek architecture, especially the use of columns and carefully planned temples, expressed values of order, balance, and beauty. Buildings on the Acropolis, including the Parthenon, showed both religious devotion and civic pride.
Greek city-states sometimes united against outside threats. The Persian Empire grew into one of the largest empires in history and controlled Greek cities in Ionia. When these cities rebelled and Athens helped them, the Persian kings tried to punish mainland Greece. Athens and Sparta formed an alliance, combining Athenian naval strength with Spartan land forces. In battles such as Marathon, Salamis, and Plataea, Greek strategies and the use of local geography helped smaller Greek forces defeat larger Persian armies. These victories protected Greek independence and encouraged many people to see themselves as part of a larger Greek world. After the wars, Athens led a naval alliance called the Delian League. Member city-states sent ships or money to a shared treasury, which helped Athens grow richer and more powerful and contributed to its golden age.
As Persian threats faded, fear and rivalry grew within Greece. Athens began to treat Delian League members less like partners and more like subjects, forcing cities such as Thasos to remain in the alliance and continue paying tribute. Sparta and allies such as Corinth formed the Peloponnesian League, claiming to defend the independence of city-states that did not want Athenian control. The Greek world split into two hostile camps, and this deep division led to the Peloponnesian War. The conflict unfolded in two long stages and drew in almost all the major city-states. Athens at first controlled the seas, but overcrowding inside its walls and a deadly plague weakened the city. Sparta later received Persian money to build a stronger navy, destroyed much of the Athenian fleet, and forced Athens to surrender. For a time, harsh pro-Spartan rulers known as the Thirty Tyrants took power in Athens. After years of fighting, many Greek city-states were left weakened and divided.
This weakness opened the way for a northern kingdom, Macedon, to dominate Greece. King Philip of Macedon built a new kind of army that trained farmers and commoners to fight in a tight phalanx formation with long spears, supported by a strong cavalry. At the Battle of Chaeronea in 338 B.C.E., Philip and his son Alexander defeated a united force from Athens and Thebes and drew most Greek city-states into a league led by Macedon. Shortly afterward, Philip was assassinated, and Alexander became king.
Alexander set out to conquer the Persian Empire and succeeded, then went on to conquer Egypt and push his armies as far as India. His troops finally refused to march farther, and he turned back, but Greek influence had already spread across a vast region. Wherever Alexander went, his armies and settlers carried Greek language, stories, art, and building styles. Local rulers and communities began to adopt Greek architecture, build theaters and marketplaces, and use Greek in government and trade. Alexander also showed interest in the cultures he encountered, studying astronomy in Mesopotamia, exploring the Nile, and collecting objects to display in museums. At the same time, he could be brutally harsh, as when he destroyed Thebes after a rebellion.
Alexander died at age 32 without a clear heir, and his generals struggled for control. After long conflicts known as the Successor Wars, his empire broke into several Hellenistic kingdoms ruled by leaders, such as Antigonus, Seleucus, and Ptolemy. They governed lands that stretched from Macedonia and the Greek city-states to Egypt, Syria, Mesopotamia, Persia, and Central Asia. Greek became the language of many royal courts and official documents. New or expanded cities such as Alexandria, Antioch, Pergamon, and Ai Khanum combined Greek plans and buildings with local traditions. The Museum of Alexandria and the Library of Alexandria gathered texts from many cultures and became centers of study. Inscriptions such as the Rosetta Stone, written in Egyptian and Greek, and translations of older writings into Greek show how widely Hellenistic culture reached.
Over time, the Hellenistic kingdoms weakened and eventually came under Roman control. Greek influence did not disappear. Instead, Roman language, art, architecture, and political ideas absorbed and reshaped Greek models.

sections.name: Lesson Plan
sections.content:
Taught with "Coming Together, Falling Apart" (Pacing: 45 minutes)
1. Begin the lesson by reviewing the essential question. Have students conduct a picture walk in their student editions.
2. Have students read the intro article "Coming Together, Falling Apart."
  A. After reading, students should write their responses to the questions at the end of the article and discuss their answers in pairs.
  B. Ask the whole class to make a prediction about how this article might relate to the part of Greek history they will study this week. (Accept all reasonable answers.)
3. Show students the video Persian Leftovers, from related media, as an introduction to who the Persian people were and who some of their leaders were. This video talks about two different examples of writing.
  A. What primary source tells us about Cyrus the Great? (the Cyrus Cylinder)
  B. What primary source tells us about the leader Darius the Great? (the Behistun Inscription, carved on the face of a cliff)
  C. Why are these sources important? (They tell us what life was like and how these men ruled the empire.)
4. Show students the video The Persian Empire, from related media. Have students listen for the ways that the empire grew. Ask:
  A. Tax money was used to create roads to connect the cities. What would the effect of these roads be? (trade, sharing cultures, movement between cities)
  B. A single form of money was used for the whole empire. What would have been the effect of everyone using the same money? (more trade)
5. Read through the first paragraph of the article "The Persian Wars" as a class. Use the image Map of Ancient Persia, from related media, to have students observe the map of the Persian Empire.
  A. Discuss: What ancient civilizations that we've learned about this year were now under the rule of the Persians? (Mesopotamia, Sumer, Babylonia, ancient Egypt, ancient Israel, the Indus River Valley, Phoenicia, Assyria)
6. Provide each student with a copy of the printable Causes and Consequences of Conflict in Ancient Greece.
  A. Students will only use the first page of the printable in this lesson. You could choose to wait to provide the second page during the next lesson.
7. Explain to students that as they read the articles and watch the videos, they should be adding information about each event to the printable. They should summarize the event, then add the cause(s) and the consequence(s) of the event as well.
8. Have students read the rest of the article.
  A. Be sure students add information to their printables.
9. Show students the video The Persian Wars from related media.
  A. Be sure students add any new information to their printables.
10. Display the images Battle of Marathon, Battle of Thermopylae, and Battle of Salamis, from related media. Have students identify the movements of the Persian army and Greek armies in each battle. Summarize each of the battles and its consequences.
  A. Be sure students add any new information to their printables.
11. Place students in small groups or pairs to read the passages "The First Marathon Runner" and "Holding the Hot Gates." Have students discuss why these battles might have been especially important.
  A. Then, ask students to discuss and write responses to the "Let's Write" prompt: How did geography affect the outcomes of the Persian Wars? (Accept all reasonable answers. Possible answer: The Greeks were able to use their knowledge of the challenging geography to their advantage.)
12. As a class, discuss what caused the Persian Wars and the consequences of the wars. (Answers may vary but could include that the Persian Wars are important to world history. People's lives might be different if the Persians had won the wars. Greek ideas like democracy and trial by jury would not have been passed down to us. With victory over the Persians, the Greeks gained power and started building an empire of their own.)
13. Have students add the Causes and Consequences of Conflicts in Ancient Greece printable to their interactive notebooks.

sections.name: Teacher Background Knowledge
sections.content:
Ancient Athens is known for its invention of democracy, which emerged after reformers challenged the rule of wealthy nobles. Cleisthenes established a council and an Assembly, giving ordinary citizens a voice. Athenian democracy flourished under Pericles, who furthered citizen participation by introducing pay for public service. Despite Athens's achievements during the golden age, Plato criticized democracy, advocating for philosopher-kings. Nevertheless, Athenian democracy's influence endures, with its principles inspiring modern democratic systems worldwide.
The Greco-Persian Wars were fought from about 492 B.C.E. to 449 B.C.E. between the small city-states of Greece and the First Persian Empire, which was one of the largest empires of all time. The wars ended with the Greeks maintaining their independence from Persia. Greeks remember the two most famous battles of the Persian Wars as evidence of their people's great courage and resourcefulness. At the Battle of Marathon in 490 B.C.E., an army from the city of Athens expelled the Persians from the Greek mainland. At the Battle of Salamis in 480 B.C.E., the superior Greek navy lured Persian ships into a strait, trapping them and ultimately destroying the Persian navy. This battle ended any hope of Persian forces taking mainland Greece.
From 460 to 446 B.C.E. and then 431 to 404 B.C.E., Athens and Sparta fought against each other in what is known as the Peloponnesian Wars. These two rival city-states fought for dominance of the southern part of the Greek peninsula, which is known as the Peloponnese. Sparta won the war, but the long conflict weakened both cities. Other parts of Greece soon came to dominate the region. First Thebes and then Macedon rose to power.

sections.name: Unit Summary
sections.content:
Ancient Greece began on a small, mountainous peninsula that stretched into the Mediterranean Sea between Europe and Asia. Steep mountains, scattered valleys, and many islands made travel difficult and kept communities apart. Villages and towns developed independently and grew into city-states, or poleis, each with its own government, laws, and identity. People thought of themselves first as citizens of places such as Athens, Sparta, or Thebes, not simply as "Greek." The sea was central to life. Limited good farmland pushed many people toward shipbuilding, sailing, fishing, and trade, so harbors and coastlines became busy centers of exchange.
Over time, different city-states experienced different types of government. Some were monarchies ruled by kings. Others became oligarchies where a small group of wealthy men shared power. At times, individuals seized control as tyrants. In Athens, reformers eventually pushed the city toward democracy. Cleisthenes reorganized Athenian society in 508 B.C.E., creating a council of 500 citizens chosen by lottery and an assembly where citizens debated and voted on laws. Later, Pericles strengthened this system by paying jurors and officials so poorer citizens could serve. Under his leadership, Athens entered a golden age of art, architecture, and philosophy. At the same time, thinkers such as Plato criticized democracy and argued that only wise, trained philosopher-kings should rule. Even with its limits and critics, Athenian democracy introduced the lasting idea that citizens should have a voice in how they are governed.
Religion shaped everyday life and public spaces. The Greeks practiced a polytheistic religion that honored many gods and goddesses who looked and acted like humans. Zeus ruled the sky with thunderbolts, Poseidon ruled the sea, and Athena represented wisdom and war. These and other deities were believed to live on Mount Olympus, yet they often stepped into human affairs. Myths told stories of the gods falling in love, arguing, taking sides in wars, and seeking revenge, which helped people understand the divine through familiar emotions. Families kept small household altars where they offered food, wine, and incense to the gods. Cities built great temples and sanctuaries, such as the Acropolis in Athens and the sanctuary of Apollo at Delphi. Festivals with processions, music, athletics, and drama, including the Panathenaia and the Olympic Games, brought communities together and reinforced a shared Greek identity. Greek religion had no single holy book; instead, myths and rituals taught moral lessons and linked worship to daily life.
Ideas also spread through philosophy and art. Socrates encouraged people to question their beliefs. Plato wrote about justice, government, and truth, and founded the Academy in Athens. Aristotle focused on observation and logic, studied science and politics, and founded the Lyceum. Their work influenced education, civic life, and ways of thinking about knowledge. Greek architecture, especially the use of columns and carefully planned temples, expressed values of order, balance, and beauty. Buildings on the Acropolis, including the Parthenon, showed both religious devotion and civic pride.
Greek city-states sometimes united against outside threats. The Persian Empire grew into one of the largest empires in history and controlled Greek cities in Ionia. When these cities rebelled and Athens helped them, the Persian kings tried to punish mainland Greece. Athens and Sparta formed an alliance, combining Athenian naval strength with Spartan land forces. In battles such as Marathon, Salamis, and Plataea, Greek strategies and the use of local geography helped smaller Greek forces defeat larger Persian armies. These victories protected Greek independence and encouraged many people to see themselves as part of a larger Greek world. After the wars, Athens led a naval alliance called the Delian League. Member city-states sent ships or money to a shared treasury, which helped Athens grow richer and more powerful and contributed to its golden age.
As Persian threats faded, fear and rivalry grew within Greece. Athens began to treat Delian League members less like partners and more like subjects, forcing cities such as Thasos to remain in the alliance and continue paying tribute. Sparta and allies such as Corinth formed the Peloponnesian League, claiming to defend the independence of city-states that did not want Athenian control. The Greek world split into two hostile camps, and this deep division led to the Peloponnesian War. The conflict unfolded in two long stages and drew in almost all the major city-states. Athens at first controlled the seas, but overcrowding inside its walls and a deadly plague weakened the city. Sparta later received Persian money to build a stronger navy, destroyed much of the Athenian fleet, and forced Athens to surrender. For a time, harsh pro-Spartan rulers known as the Thirty Tyrants took power in Athens. After years of fighting, many Greek city-states were left weakened and divided.
This weakness opened the way for a northern kingdom, Macedon, to dominate Greece. King Philip of Macedon built a new kind of army that trained farmers and commoners to fight in a tight phalanx formation with long spears, supported by a strong cavalry. At the Battle of Chaeronea in 338 B.C.E., Philip and his son Alexander defeated a united force from Athens and Thebes and drew most Greek city-states into a league led by Macedon. Shortly afterward, Philip was assassinated, and Alexander became king.
Alexander set out to conquer the Persian Empire and succeeded, then went on to conquer Egypt and push his armies as far as India. His troops finally refused to march farther, and he turned back, but Greek influence had already spread across a vast region. Wherever Alexander went, his armies and settlers carried Greek language, stories, art, and building styles. Local rulers and communities began to adopt Greek architecture, build theaters and marketplaces, and use Greek in government and trade. Alexander also showed interest in the cultures he encountered, studying astronomy in Mesopotamia, exploring the Nile, and collecting objects to display in museums. At the same time, he could be brutally harsh, as when he destroyed Thebes after a rebellion.
Alexander died at age 32 without a clear heir, and his generals struggled for control. After long conflicts known as the Successor Wars, his empire broke into several Hellenistic kingdoms ruled by leaders, such as Antigonus, Seleucus, and Ptolemy. They governed lands that stretched from Macedonia and the Greek city-states to Egypt, Syria, Mesopotamia, Persia, and Central Asia. Greek became the language of many royal courts and official documents. New or expanded cities such as Alexandria, Antioch, Pergamon, and Ai Khanum combined Greek plans and buildings with local traditions. The Museum of Alexandria and the Library of Alexandria gathered texts from many cultures and became centers of study. Inscriptions such as the Rosetta Stone, written in Egyptian and Greek, and translations of older writings into Greek show how widely Hellenistic culture reached.
Over time, the Hellenistic kingdoms weakened and eventually came under Roman control. Greek influence did not disappear. Instead, Roman language, art, architecture, and political ideas absorbed and reshaped Greek models.

sections.name: Let’s Write
sections.content:
In what important ways were Athens and Sparta different from each other, and how did those differences shape the history of Greece?
How did the ideas of philosophers such as Socrates, Plato, and Aristotle challenge people in ancient Greece to think differently about government?
How did the Persian Wars and the Peloponnesian War each change the balance of power among Greek city-states and affect the future of Greece?

sections.name: Apply What You Learned
sections.content:
Choose one of the activities to show what you've learned:
WRITE:
Which city-state in ancient Greece do you think you would have wanted to live in, such as Athens, Sparta, or another polis? Write one or two paragraphs explaining your choice and the reasons for your choice, using facts you have learned.
Was Alexander the Great truly "great"? Write 1-2 paragraphs explaining your opinion using examples from his conquests and how he treated the places he ruled.
Which physical feature of Greece, such as mountains, islands, or seas, do you think had the biggest effect on how people lived? Write 1-2 paragraphs explaining your choice, and use specific examples to show how that feature affected travel, trade, or farming.
RESEARCH/PRESENT:
Choose one Greek god, goddess, or hero you would like to learn more about. Create a poster or a presentation explaining what Greek people believed about this deity and how the people worshipped the deity.
Research a famous Greek building, such as the Parthenon, or a temple from another city-state. Find out what it was used for and what its main features were. Create a labeled drawing or model and present at least three facts about the building.
SPEAK:
Work in pairs to discuss what you think was the most important idea the Greeks contributed to the world (for example, democracy, drama, philosophy, or architecture). Explain your choice, and listen to what your partner thinks. Use reasoning and examples to defend your view.
Choose a Greek myth you have learned about. Work in pairs and retell the myth in your own words, making sure to explain what lesson or message the myth teaches. Then listen to your partner's myth and ask questions about it.
Write and deliver a speech from the perspective of Pericles, urging the citizens of Athens to take pride in their democratic government. Your speech should accurately describe the features and rules of Athenian democracy.
PLAN:
Work in pairs to design a mini Greek festival for your class. Plan activities taken from ancient Greek culture, including at least one activity involving food, one involving games or sports, and one involving arts or theater. Make a list of what you would need and how you would decorate.
Work in a small group to plan a short play based on a Greek myth or historical event, such as the Battle of Marathon or life in Athens. Decide on characters, scenes, and simple props. Write a brief outline of what each person will do.
Work with a partner to design a student government based loosely on Athenian democracy. Decide who can vote, how leaders are chosen, and how decisions will be made. Write down your rules and be ready to explain why you chose them.
DRAW:
Draw a map of ancient Greece showing the surrounding seas, mountains, and at least three important city-states. Label each part and add a key. On another piece of paper, write a paragraph explaining how geography might affect those city-states
Draw a scene from daily life in either Athens or Sparta. Include buildings, clothing, and activities that match what you have learned. Be ready to explain what each part of your picture shows.
PODCAST: Choose a podcast from this unit. Listen to the podcast individually or in pairs.
Draw a picture to represent what you learned.
Write a list of things you learned.
Create 3-5 questions from the podcast.
Create a graphic organizer from the podcast content.

sections.name: Lesson Plan
sections.content:
Taught with "The Peloponnesian War" and "Primary Source Analysis: Pericles's Funeral Oration" (Pacing: 60 minutes)
1. Have students retrieve the Causes and Consequences of Conflict in Ancient Greece printable from their interactive notebooks.
  A. Provide students with the second page of the printable if you did not in the previous lesson.
2. As a class, discuss the following questions:
  A. What were the two sides in the Persian Wars? (the Persian Empire and the allied Greek city-states)
  B. Would you describe the Persian Wars as an internal or external conflict? (It was an external conflict. The Persian Empire was an external empire, as it was not part of ancient Greece.)
    A. If necessary, define "external" and "internal."
  C. What internal conflicts do you predict might occur in ancient Greece? (Accept all reasonable responses.)
3. Have the students read the article "Political Leagues."
  A. Ensure that students are completing the second page of the Causes and Consequences of Conflicts in Ancient Greece printable as they read.
  B. Differentiation: Read the article out loud, and model highlighting and underlining reasons for the split between political leagues.
4. Introduce the primary source activity "Perspectives From the Past" by telling students that they will use quotes collected by an ancient historian to explain how the cooperation between Greek city-states began to fall apart.
  A. Students should work in pairs to read and paraphrase each quote. Then, students will independently write about the perspectives and motivations of the Delian and Peloponnesian Leagues.
5. Before reading "The Peloponnesian War," ask students to share what they believe caused the war to begin. (Conflict within the Delian League about the behavior of Athens eventually grew into a war between two groups of city-states.)
6. Have students read "The Peloponnesian War" in pairs or small groups.
  A. Ensure that students are completing the second page of the Causes and Consequences of Conflicts in Ancient Greece printable as they read.
7. Show students the video The Peloponnesian War 431-404 B.C.E., from related media.
  A. Be sure students add any new information to their printables.
8. Have each pair or small group get with another group or pair and compare printables. Have students add any missing information or corrections as they share.
  A. Have students add the second page of the printable to their interactive notebooks if they have not done so already.
  B. Extension activity: Have students research battles of the Peloponnesian Wars and strategies used by each side. Have students create a poster showing the strengths and weaknesses of each side during the war.
9. Conduct a class picture walk of the image Pericles Funeral Oration, found in related media. Have students answer these analysis questions in pairs or small groups:
  A. What is happening in the painting?
  B. What do you notice?
  C. What do you think the title means?
10. Look at the article "Primary Source Analysis: Pericles's Funeral Oration" as a class. Read the first paragraph as a class.
11. Then, read the rest of the article "Primary Source: Pericles's Funeral Oration," one paragraph at a time, as a class. Stop after each paragraph to discuss the analysis questions. Review students' responses as a class.
  A. Ask:
    A. What is the main idea of the text?
    B. Rewrite the passage in your own words.
  B. As students read the excerpts, stop and clarify any words they do not understand.
  C. Encourage students to read the excerpt multiple times for clarity.
  D. Differentiation: Lead students through finding the main idea, and rewrite the first excerpt in your own words before having students do it themselves for the second excerpt.
12. Students will use the article "Primary Source Analysis: Pericles's Funeral Oration" to write a claim and provide evidence and an explanation to support their claim.

sections.name: Teacher Background Knowledge
sections.content:
Ancient Athens is known for its invention of democracy, which emerged after reformers challenged the rule of wealthy nobles. Cleisthenes established a council and an Assembly, giving ordinary citizens a voice. Athenian democracy flourished under Pericles, who furthered citizen participation by introducing pay for public service. Despite Athens's achievements during the golden age, Plato criticized democracy, advocating for philosopher-kings. Nevertheless, Athenian democracy's influence endures, with its principles inspiring modern democratic systems worldwide.
The Greco-Persian Wars were fought from about 492 B.C.E. to 449 B.C.E. between the small city-states of Greece and the First Persian Empire, which was one of the largest empires of all time. The wars ended with the Greeks maintaining their independence from Persia. Greeks remember the two most famous battles of the Persian Wars as evidence of their people's great courage and resourcefulness. At the Battle of Marathon in 490 B.C.E., an army from the city of Athens expelled the Persians from the Greek mainland. At the Battle of Salamis in 480 B.C.E., the superior Greek navy lured Persian ships into a strait, trapping them and ultimately destroying the Persian navy. This battle ended any hope of Persian forces taking mainland Greece.
From 460 to 446 B.C.E. and then 431 to 404 B.C.E., Athens and Sparta fought against each other in what is known as the Peloponnesian Wars. These two rival city-states fought for dominance of the southern part of the Greek peninsula, which is known as the Peloponnese. Sparta won the war, but the long conflict weakened both cities. Other parts of Greece soon came to dominate the region. First Thebes and then Macedon rose to power.

sections.name: Unit Summary
sections.content:
Ancient Greece began on a small, mountainous peninsula that stretched into the Mediterranean Sea between Europe and Asia. Steep mountains, scattered valleys, and many islands made travel difficult and kept communities apart. Villages and towns developed independently and grew into city-states, or poleis, each with its own government, laws, and identity. People thought of themselves first as citizens of places such as Athens, Sparta, or Thebes, not simply as "Greek." The sea was central to life. Limited good farmland pushed many people toward shipbuilding, sailing, fishing, and trade, so harbors and coastlines became busy centers of exchange.
Over time, different city-states experienced different types of government. Some were monarchies ruled by kings. Others became oligarchies where a small group of wealthy men shared power. At times, individuals seized control as tyrants. In Athens, reformers eventually pushed the city toward democracy. Cleisthenes reorganized Athenian society in 508 B.C.E., creating a council of 500 citizens chosen by lottery and an assembly where citizens debated and voted on laws. Later, Pericles strengthened this system by paying jurors and officials so poorer citizens could serve. Under his leadership, Athens entered a golden age of art, architecture, and philosophy. At the same time, thinkers such as Plato criticized democracy and argued that only wise, trained philosopher-kings should rule. Even with its limits and critics, Athenian democracy introduced the lasting idea that citizens should have a voice in how they are governed.
Religion shaped everyday life and public spaces. The Greeks practiced a polytheistic religion that honored many gods and goddesses who looked and acted like humans. Zeus ruled the sky with thunderbolts, Poseidon ruled the sea, and Athena represented wisdom and war. These and other deities were believed to live on Mount Olympus, yet they often stepped into human affairs. Myths told stories of the gods falling in love, arguing, taking sides in wars, and seeking revenge, which helped people understand the divine through familiar emotions. Families kept small household altars where they offered food, wine, and incense to the gods. Cities built great temples and sanctuaries, such as the Acropolis in Athens and the sanctuary of Apollo at Delphi. Festivals with processions, music, athletics, and drama, including the Panathenaia and the Olympic Games, brought communities together and reinforced a shared Greek identity. Greek religion had no single holy book; instead, myths and rituals taught moral lessons and linked worship to daily life.
Ideas also spread through philosophy and art. Socrates encouraged people to question their beliefs. Plato wrote about justice, government, and truth, and founded the Academy in Athens. Aristotle focused on observation and logic, studied science and politics, and founded the Lyceum. Their work influenced education, civic life, and ways of thinking about knowledge. Greek architecture, especially the use of columns and carefully planned temples, expressed values of order, balance, and beauty. Buildings on the Acropolis, including the Parthenon, showed both religious devotion and civic pride.
Greek city-states sometimes united against outside threats. The Persian Empire grew into one of the largest empires in history and controlled Greek cities in Ionia. When these cities rebelled and Athens helped them, the Persian kings tried to punish mainland Greece. Athens and Sparta formed an alliance, combining Athenian naval strength with Spartan land forces. In battles such as Marathon, Salamis, and Plataea, Greek strategies and the use of local geography helped smaller Greek forces defeat larger Persian armies. These victories protected Greek independence and encouraged many people to see themselves as part of a larger Greek world. After the wars, Athens led a naval alliance called the Delian League. Member city-states sent ships or money to a shared treasury, which helped Athens grow richer and more powerful and contributed to its golden age.
As Persian threats faded, fear and rivalry grew within Greece. Athens began to treat Delian League members less like partners and more like subjects, forcing cities such as Thasos to remain in the alliance and continue paying tribute. Sparta and allies such as Corinth formed the Peloponnesian League, claiming to defend the independence of city-states that did not want Athenian control. The Greek world split into two hostile camps, and this deep division led to the Peloponnesian War. The conflict unfolded in two long stages and drew in almost all the major city-states. Athens at first controlled the seas, but overcrowding inside its walls and a deadly plague weakened the city. Sparta later received Persian money to build a stronger navy, destroyed much of the Athenian fleet, and forced Athens to surrender. For a time, harsh pro-Spartan rulers known as the Thirty Tyrants took power in Athens. After years of fighting, many Greek city-states were left weakened and divided.
This weakness opened the way for a northern kingdom, Macedon, to dominate Greece. King Philip of Macedon built a new kind of army that trained farmers and commoners to fight in a tight phalanx formation with long spears, supported by a strong cavalry. At the Battle of Chaeronea in 338 B.C.E., Philip and his son Alexander defeated a united force from Athens and Thebes and drew most Greek city-states into a league led by Macedon. Shortly afterward, Philip was assassinated, and Alexander became king.
Alexander set out to conquer the Persian Empire and succeeded, then went on to conquer Egypt and push his armies as far as India. His troops finally refused to march farther, and he turned back, but Greek influence had already spread across a vast region. Wherever Alexander went, his armies and settlers carried Greek language, stories, art, and building styles. Local rulers and communities began to adopt Greek architecture, build theaters and marketplaces, and use Greek in government and trade. Alexander also showed interest in the cultures he encountered, studying astronomy in Mesopotamia, exploring the Nile, and collecting objects to display in museums. At the same time, he could be brutally harsh, as when he destroyed Thebes after a rebellion.
Alexander died at age 32 without a clear heir, and his generals struggled for control. After long conflicts known as the Successor Wars, his empire broke into several Hellenistic kingdoms ruled by leaders, such as Antigonus, Seleucus, and Ptolemy. They governed lands that stretched from Macedonia and the Greek city-states to Egypt, Syria, Mesopotamia, Persia, and Central Asia. Greek became the language of many royal courts and official documents. New or expanded cities such as Alexandria, Antioch, Pergamon, and Ai Khanum combined Greek plans and buildings with local traditions. The Museum of Alexandria and the Library of Alexandria gathered texts from many cultures and became centers of study. Inscriptions such as the Rosetta Stone, written in Egyptian and Greek, and translations of older writings into Greek show how widely Hellenistic culture reached.
Over time, the Hellenistic kingdoms weakened and eventually came under Roman control. Greek influence did not disappear. Instead, Roman language, art, architecture, and political ideas absorbed and reshaped Greek models.
