sections.name: Let’s Write
sections.content:
In what important ways were Athens and Sparta different from each other, and how did those differences shape the history of Greece?
How did the ideas of philosophers such as Socrates, Plato, and Aristotle challenge people in ancient Greece to think differently about government?
How did the Persian Wars and the Peloponnesian War each change the balance of power among Greek city-states and affect the future of Greece?

sections.name: Apply What You Learned
sections.content:
Choose one of the activities to show what you've learned:
WRITE:
Which city-state in ancient Greece do you think you would have wanted to live in, such as Athens, Sparta, or another polis? Write one or two paragraphs explaining your choice and the reasons for your choice, using facts you have learned.
Was Alexander the Great truly "great"? Write 1-2 paragraphs explaining your opinion using examples from his conquests and how he treated the places he ruled.
Which physical feature of Greece, such as mountains, islands, or seas, do you think had the biggest effect on how people lived? Write 1-2 paragraphs explaining your choice, and use specific examples to show how that feature affected travel, trade, or farming.
RESEARCH/PRESENT:
Choose one Greek god, goddess, or hero you would like to learn more about. Create a poster or a presentation explaining what Greek people believed about this deity and how the people worshipped the deity.
Research a famous Greek building, such as the Parthenon, or a temple from another city-state. Find out what it was used for and what its main features were. Create a labeled drawing or model and present at least three facts about the building.
SPEAK:
Work in pairs to discuss what you think was the most important idea the Greeks contributed to the world (for example, democracy, drama, philosophy, or architecture). Explain your choice, and listen to what your partner thinks. Use reasoning and examples to defend your view.
Choose a Greek myth you have learned about. Work in pairs and retell the myth in your own words, making sure to explain what lesson or message the myth teaches. Then listen to your partner's myth and ask questions about it.
Write and deliver a speech from the perspective of Pericles, urging the citizens of Athens to take pride in their democratic government. Your speech should accurately describe the features and rules of Athenian democracy.
PLAN:
Work in pairs to design a mini Greek festival for your class. Plan activities taken from ancient Greek culture, including at least one activity involving food, one involving games or sports, and one involving arts or theater. Make a list of what you would need and how you would decorate.
Work in a small group to plan a short play based on a Greek myth or historical event, such as the Battle of Marathon or life in Athens. Decide on characters, scenes, and simple props. Write a brief outline of what each person will do.
Work with a partner to design a student government based loosely on Athenian democracy. Decide who can vote, how leaders are chosen, and how decisions will be made. Write down your rules and be ready to explain why you chose them.
DRAW:
Draw a map of ancient Greece showing the surrounding seas, mountains, and at least three important city-states. Label each part and add a key. On another piece of paper, write a paragraph explaining how geography might affect those city-states
Draw a scene from daily life in either Athens or Sparta. Include buildings, clothing, and activities that match what you have learned. Be ready to explain what each part of your picture shows.
PODCAST: Choose a podcast from this unit. Listen to the podcast individually or in pairs.
Draw a picture to represent what you learned.
Write a list of things you learned.
Create 3-5 questions from the podcast.
Create a graphic organizer from the podcast content.

sections.name: Unit Summary
sections.content:
Ancient Greece began on a small, mountainous peninsula that stretched into the Mediterranean Sea between Europe and Asia. Steep mountains, scattered valleys, and many islands made travel difficult and kept communities apart. Villages and towns developed independently and grew into city-states, or poleis, each with its own government, laws, and identity. People thought of themselves first as citizens of places such as Athens, Sparta, or Thebes, not simply as "Greek." The sea was central to life. Limited good farmland pushed many people toward shipbuilding, sailing, fishing, and trade, so harbors and coastlines became busy centers of exchange.
Over time, different city-states experienced different types of government. Some were monarchies ruled by kings. Others became oligarchies where a small group of wealthy men shared power. At times, individuals seized control as tyrants. In Athens, reformers eventually pushed the city toward democracy. Cleisthenes reorganized Athenian society in 508 B.C.E., creating a council of 500 citizens chosen by lottery and an assembly where citizens debated and voted on laws. Later, Pericles strengthened this system by paying jurors and officials so poorer citizens could serve. Under his leadership, Athens entered a golden age of art, architecture, and philosophy. At the same time, thinkers such as Plato criticized democracy and argued that only wise, trained philosopher-kings should rule. Even with its limits and critics, Athenian democracy introduced the lasting idea that citizens should have a voice in how they are governed.
Religion shaped everyday life and public spaces. The Greeks practiced a polytheistic religion that honored many gods and goddesses who looked and acted like humans. Zeus ruled the sky with thunderbolts, Poseidon ruled the sea, and Athena represented wisdom and war. These and other deities were believed to live on Mount Olympus, yet they often stepped into human affairs. Myths told stories of the gods falling in love, arguing, taking sides in wars, and seeking revenge, which helped people understand the divine through familiar emotions. Families kept small household altars where they offered food, wine, and incense to the gods. Cities built great temples and sanctuaries, such as the Acropolis in Athens and the sanctuary of Apollo at Delphi. Festivals with processions, music, athletics, and drama, including the Panathenaia and the Olympic Games, brought communities together and reinforced a shared Greek identity. Greek religion had no single holy book; instead, myths and rituals taught moral lessons and linked worship to daily life.
Ideas also spread through philosophy and art. Socrates encouraged people to question their beliefs. Plato wrote about justice, government, and truth, and founded the Academy in Athens. Aristotle focused on observation and logic, studied science and politics, and founded the Lyceum. Their work influenced education, civic life, and ways of thinking about knowledge. Greek architecture, especially the use of columns and carefully planned temples, expressed values of order, balance, and beauty. Buildings on the Acropolis, including the Parthenon, showed both religious devotion and civic pride.
Greek city-states sometimes united against outside threats. The Persian Empire grew into one of the largest empires in history and controlled Greek cities in Ionia. When these cities rebelled and Athens helped them, the Persian kings tried to punish mainland Greece. Athens and Sparta formed an alliance, combining Athenian naval strength with Spartan land forces. In battles such as Marathon, Salamis, and Plataea, Greek strategies and the use of local geography helped smaller Greek forces defeat larger Persian armies. These victories protected Greek independence and encouraged many people to see themselves as part of a larger Greek world. After the wars, Athens led a naval alliance called the Delian League. Member city-states sent ships or money to a shared treasury, which helped Athens grow richer and more powerful and contributed to its golden age.
As Persian threats faded, fear and rivalry grew within Greece. Athens began to treat Delian League members less like partners and more like subjects, forcing cities such as Thasos to remain in the alliance and continue paying tribute. Sparta and allies such as Corinth formed the Peloponnesian League, claiming to defend the independence of city-states that did not want Athenian control. The Greek world split into two hostile camps, and this deep division led to the Peloponnesian War. The conflict unfolded in two long stages and drew in almost all the major city-states. Athens at first controlled the seas, but overcrowding inside its walls and a deadly plague weakened the city. Sparta later received Persian money to build a stronger navy, destroyed much of the Athenian fleet, and forced Athens to surrender. For a time, harsh pro-Spartan rulers known as the Thirty Tyrants took power in Athens. After years of fighting, many Greek city-states were left weakened and divided.
This weakness opened the way for a northern kingdom, Macedon, to dominate Greece. King Philip of Macedon built a new kind of army that trained farmers and commoners to fight in a tight phalanx formation with long spears, supported by a strong cavalry. At the Battle of Chaeronea in 338 B.C.E., Philip and his son Alexander defeated a united force from Athens and Thebes and drew most Greek city-states into a league led by Macedon. Shortly afterward, Philip was assassinated, and Alexander became king.
Alexander set out to conquer the Persian Empire and succeeded, then went on to conquer Egypt and push his armies as far as India. His troops finally refused to march farther, and he turned back, but Greek influence had already spread across a vast region. Wherever Alexander went, his armies and settlers carried Greek language, stories, art, and building styles. Local rulers and communities began to adopt Greek architecture, build theaters and marketplaces, and use Greek in government and trade. Alexander also showed interest in the cultures he encountered, studying astronomy in Mesopotamia, exploring the Nile, and collecting objects to display in museums. At the same time, he could be brutally harsh, as when he destroyed Thebes after a rebellion.
Alexander died at age 32 without a clear heir, and his generals struggled for control. After long conflicts known as the Successor Wars, his empire broke into several Hellenistic kingdoms ruled by leaders, such as Antigonus, Seleucus, and Ptolemy. They governed lands that stretched from Macedonia and the Greek city-states to Egypt, Syria, Mesopotamia, Persia, and Central Asia. Greek became the language of many royal courts and official documents. New or expanded cities such as Alexandria, Antioch, Pergamon, and Ai Khanum combined Greek plans and buildings with local traditions. The Museum of Alexandria and the Library of Alexandria gathered texts from many cultures and became centers of study. Inscriptions such as the Rosetta Stone, written in Egyptian and Greek, and translations of older writings into Greek show how widely Hellenistic culture reached.
Over time, the Hellenistic kingdoms weakened and eventually came under Roman control. Greek influence did not disappear. Instead, Roman language, art, architecture, and political ideas absorbed and reshaped Greek models.

sections.name: Let’s Write
sections.content:
In what important ways were Athens and Sparta different from each other, and how did those differences shape the history of Greece?
How did the ideas of philosophers such as Socrates, Plato, and Aristotle challenge people in ancient Greece to think differently about government?
How did the Persian Wars and the Peloponnesian War each change the balance of power among Greek city-states and affect the future of Greece?

sections.name: Apply What You Learned
sections.content:
Choose one of the activities to show what you've learned:
WRITE:
Which city-state in ancient Greece do you think you would have wanted to live in, such as Athens, Sparta, or another polis? Write one or two paragraphs explaining your choice and the reasons for your choice, using facts you have learned.
Was Alexander the Great truly "great"? Write 1-2 paragraphs explaining your opinion using examples from his conquests and how he treated the places he ruled.
Which physical feature of Greece, such as mountains, islands, or seas, do you think had the biggest effect on how people lived? Write 1-2 paragraphs explaining your choice, and use specific examples to show how that feature affected travel, trade, or farming.
RESEARCH/PRESENT:
Choose one Greek god, goddess, or hero you would like to learn more about. Create a poster or a presentation explaining what Greek people believed about this deity and how the people worshipped the deity.
Research a famous Greek building, such as the Parthenon, or a temple from another city-state. Find out what it was used for and what its main features were. Create a labeled drawing or model and present at least three facts about the building.
SPEAK:
Work in pairs to discuss what you think was the most important idea the Greeks contributed to the world (for example, democracy, drama, philosophy, or architecture). Explain your choice, and listen to what your partner thinks. Use reasoning and examples to defend your view.
Choose a Greek myth you have learned about. Work in pairs and retell the myth in your own words, making sure to explain what lesson or message the myth teaches. Then listen to your partner's myth and ask questions about it.
Write and deliver a speech from the perspective of Pericles, urging the citizens of Athens to take pride in their democratic government. Your speech should accurately describe the features and rules of Athenian democracy.
PLAN:
Work in pairs to design a mini Greek festival for your class. Plan activities taken from ancient Greek culture, including at least one activity involving food, one involving games or sports, and one involving arts or theater. Make a list of what you would need and how you would decorate.
Work in a small group to plan a short play based on a Greek myth or historical event, such as the Battle of Marathon or life in Athens. Decide on characters, scenes, and simple props. Write a brief outline of what each person will do.
Work with a partner to design a student government based loosely on Athenian democracy. Decide who can vote, how leaders are chosen, and how decisions will be made. Write down your rules and be ready to explain why you chose them.
DRAW:
Draw a map of ancient Greece showing the surrounding seas, mountains, and at least three important city-states. Label each part and add a key. On another piece of paper, write a paragraph explaining how geography might affect those city-states
Draw a scene from daily life in either Athens or Sparta. Include buildings, clothing, and activities that match what you have learned. Be ready to explain what each part of your picture shows.
PODCAST: Choose a podcast from this unit. Listen to the podcast individually or in pairs.
Draw a picture to represent what you learned.
Write a list of things you learned.
Create 3-5 questions from the podcast.
Create a graphic organizer from the podcast content.

sections.name: Unit Summary
sections.content:
Ancient Greece began on a small, mountainous peninsula that stretched into the Mediterranean Sea between Europe and Asia. Steep mountains, scattered valleys, and many islands made travel difficult and kept communities apart. Villages and towns developed independently and grew into city-states, or poleis, each with its own government, laws, and identity. People thought of themselves first as citizens of places such as Athens, Sparta, or Thebes, not simply as "Greek." The sea was central to life. Limited good farmland pushed many people toward shipbuilding, sailing, fishing, and trade, so harbors and coastlines became busy centers of exchange.
Over time, different city-states experienced different types of government. Some were monarchies ruled by kings. Others became oligarchies where a small group of wealthy men shared power. At times, individuals seized control as tyrants. In Athens, reformers eventually pushed the city toward democracy. Cleisthenes reorganized Athenian society in 508 B.C.E., creating a council of 500 citizens chosen by lottery and an assembly where citizens debated and voted on laws. Later, Pericles strengthened this system by paying jurors and officials so poorer citizens could serve. Under his leadership, Athens entered a golden age of art, architecture, and philosophy. At the same time, thinkers such as Plato criticized democracy and argued that only wise, trained philosopher-kings should rule. Even with its limits and critics, Athenian democracy introduced the lasting idea that citizens should have a voice in how they are governed.
Religion shaped everyday life and public spaces. The Greeks practiced a polytheistic religion that honored many gods and goddesses who looked and acted like humans. Zeus ruled the sky with thunderbolts, Poseidon ruled the sea, and Athena represented wisdom and war. These and other deities were believed to live on Mount Olympus, yet they often stepped into human affairs. Myths told stories of the gods falling in love, arguing, taking sides in wars, and seeking revenge, which helped people understand the divine through familiar emotions. Families kept small household altars where they offered food, wine, and incense to the gods. Cities built great temples and sanctuaries, such as the Acropolis in Athens and the sanctuary of Apollo at Delphi. Festivals with processions, music, athletics, and drama, including the Panathenaia and the Olympic Games, brought communities together and reinforced a shared Greek identity. Greek religion had no single holy book; instead, myths and rituals taught moral lessons and linked worship to daily life.
Ideas also spread through philosophy and art. Socrates encouraged people to question their beliefs. Plato wrote about justice, government, and truth, and founded the Academy in Athens. Aristotle focused on observation and logic, studied science and politics, and founded the Lyceum. Their work influenced education, civic life, and ways of thinking about knowledge. Greek architecture, especially the use of columns and carefully planned temples, expressed values of order, balance, and beauty. Buildings on the Acropolis, including the Parthenon, showed both religious devotion and civic pride.
Greek city-states sometimes united against outside threats. The Persian Empire grew into one of the largest empires in history and controlled Greek cities in Ionia. When these cities rebelled and Athens helped them, the Persian kings tried to punish mainland Greece. Athens and Sparta formed an alliance, combining Athenian naval strength with Spartan land forces. In battles such as Marathon, Salamis, and Plataea, Greek strategies and the use of local geography helped smaller Greek forces defeat larger Persian armies. These victories protected Greek independence and encouraged many people to see themselves as part of a larger Greek world. After the wars, Athens led a naval alliance called the Delian League. Member city-states sent ships or money to a shared treasury, which helped Athens grow richer and more powerful and contributed to its golden age.
As Persian threats faded, fear and rivalry grew within Greece. Athens began to treat Delian League members less like partners and more like subjects, forcing cities such as Thasos to remain in the alliance and continue paying tribute. Sparta and allies such as Corinth formed the Peloponnesian League, claiming to defend the independence of city-states that did not want Athenian control. The Greek world split into two hostile camps, and this deep division led to the Peloponnesian War. The conflict unfolded in two long stages and drew in almost all the major city-states. Athens at first controlled the seas, but overcrowding inside its walls and a deadly plague weakened the city. Sparta later received Persian money to build a stronger navy, destroyed much of the Athenian fleet, and forced Athens to surrender. For a time, harsh pro-Spartan rulers known as the Thirty Tyrants took power in Athens. After years of fighting, many Greek city-states were left weakened and divided.
This weakness opened the way for a northern kingdom, Macedon, to dominate Greece. King Philip of Macedon built a new kind of army that trained farmers and commoners to fight in a tight phalanx formation with long spears, supported by a strong cavalry. At the Battle of Chaeronea in 338 B.C.E., Philip and his son Alexander defeated a united force from Athens and Thebes and drew most Greek city-states into a league led by Macedon. Shortly afterward, Philip was assassinated, and Alexander became king.
Alexander set out to conquer the Persian Empire and succeeded, then went on to conquer Egypt and push his armies as far as India. His troops finally refused to march farther, and he turned back, but Greek influence had already spread across a vast region. Wherever Alexander went, his armies and settlers carried Greek language, stories, art, and building styles. Local rulers and communities began to adopt Greek architecture, build theaters and marketplaces, and use Greek in government and trade. Alexander also showed interest in the cultures he encountered, studying astronomy in Mesopotamia, exploring the Nile, and collecting objects to display in museums. At the same time, he could be brutally harsh, as when he destroyed Thebes after a rebellion.
Alexander died at age 32 without a clear heir, and his generals struggled for control. After long conflicts known as the Successor Wars, his empire broke into several Hellenistic kingdoms ruled by leaders, such as Antigonus, Seleucus, and Ptolemy. They governed lands that stretched from Macedonia and the Greek city-states to Egypt, Syria, Mesopotamia, Persia, and Central Asia. Greek became the language of many royal courts and official documents. New or expanded cities such as Alexandria, Antioch, Pergamon, and Ai Khanum combined Greek plans and buildings with local traditions. The Museum of Alexandria and the Library of Alexandria gathered texts from many cultures and became centers of study. Inscriptions such as the Rosetta Stone, written in Egyptian and Greek, and translations of older writings into Greek show how widely Hellenistic culture reached.
Over time, the Hellenistic kingdoms weakened and eventually came under Roman control. Greek influence did not disappear. Instead, Roman language, art, architecture, and political ideas absorbed and reshaped Greek models.

sections.name: Let’s Write
sections.content:
In what important ways were Athens and Sparta different from each other, and how did those differences shape the history of Greece?
How did the ideas of philosophers such as Socrates, Plato, and Aristotle challenge people in ancient Greece to think differently about government?
How did the Persian Wars and the Peloponnesian War each change the balance of power among Greek city-states and affect the future of Greece?

sections.name: Apply What You Learned
sections.content:
Choose one of the activities to show what you've learned:
WRITE:
Which city-state in ancient Greece do you think you would have wanted to live in, such as Athens, Sparta, or another polis? Write one or two paragraphs explaining your choice and the reasons for your choice, using facts you have learned.
Was Alexander the Great truly "great"? Write 1-2 paragraphs explaining your opinion using examples from his conquests and how he treated the places he ruled.
Which physical feature of Greece, such as mountains, islands, or seas, do you think had the biggest effect on how people lived? Write 1-2 paragraphs explaining your choice, and use specific examples to show how that feature affected travel, trade, or farming.
RESEARCH/PRESENT:
Choose one Greek god, goddess, or hero you would like to learn more about. Create a poster or a presentation explaining what Greek people believed about this deity and how the people worshipped the deity.
Research a famous Greek building, such as the Parthenon, or a temple from another city-state. Find out what it was used for and what its main features were. Create a labeled drawing or model and present at least three facts about the building.
SPEAK:
Work in pairs to discuss what you think was the most important idea the Greeks contributed to the world (for example, democracy, drama, philosophy, or architecture). Explain your choice, and listen to what your partner thinks. Use reasoning and examples to defend your view.
Choose a Greek myth you have learned about. Work in pairs and retell the myth in your own words, making sure to explain what lesson or message the myth teaches. Then listen to your partner's myth and ask questions about it.
Write and deliver a speech from the perspective of Pericles, urging the citizens of Athens to take pride in their democratic government. Your speech should accurately describe the features and rules of Athenian democracy.
PLAN:
Work in pairs to design a mini Greek festival for your class. Plan activities taken from ancient Greek culture, including at least one activity involving food, one involving games or sports, and one involving arts or theater. Make a list of what you would need and how you would decorate.
Work in a small group to plan a short play based on a Greek myth or historical event, such as the Battle of Marathon or life in Athens. Decide on characters, scenes, and simple props. Write a brief outline of what each person will do.
Work with a partner to design a student government based loosely on Athenian democracy. Decide who can vote, how leaders are chosen, and how decisions will be made. Write down your rules and be ready to explain why you chose them.
DRAW:
Draw a map of ancient Greece showing the surrounding seas, mountains, and at least three important city-states. Label each part and add a key. On another piece of paper, write a paragraph explaining how geography might affect those city-states
Draw a scene from daily life in either Athens or Sparta. Include buildings, clothing, and activities that match what you have learned. Be ready to explain what each part of your picture shows.
PODCAST: Choose a podcast from this unit. Listen to the podcast individually or in pairs.
Draw a picture to represent what you learned.
Write a list of things you learned.
Create 3-5 questions from the podcast.
Create a graphic organizer from the podcast content.

tr_articles.title: Activity 5: Effects of Wars in the Mediterranean World

sections.name: Lesson Plan
sections.content:
Pacing: 60 minutes
1. Have students bring out their Causes and Consequences of Conflict in Ancient Greece printables.
2. Using their notes in the printables, have students discuss the following questions in pairs:
  A. What were the causes of the Persian Wars? (Athens and Sparta supported Greek city-states' rebellion against the Persian Empire. The Persian Empire wanted to control the Greek city-states.)
  B. What were the consequences of the Persian Wars? (The allied Greek forces won, showing that together, they could defeat the greatest army in the world.)
  C. What were the causes of the Peloponnesian Wars? (the rivalry between Athens and Sparta, the growing power of the Athenian-led Delian League, and the formation of the Peloponnesian League by Sparta and Corinth)
  D. What were the consequences of the Peloponnesian Wars? (Sparta won, but the Greek city-states were weakened after decades of war.)
  E. What do you predict happened to the Greek city-states after they were weakened by wars? (Accept all reasonable answers.)
3. Display the image Effects of Wars in the Mediterranean World, from related media. Discuss the following questions as a class:
  A. What do you notice in the maps? (The first map shows the Greek city-states and tribal areas before the Persian Wars. The second map shows the Macedonian kingdom around 336 B.C.E., about 70 years after the Peloponnesian Wars. The Macedonian kingdom covered a lot of areas that were once Greek city-states or tribal areas.)
  B. What do you think caused the differences between the maps? (Answers will vary but could include that the city-states being weakened after the Peloponnesian Wars made it easier for other areas, like Macedon, to take control.)
4. Have students answer the questions in their student editions.
5. Explain to students that they will write a response to the week's essential question: How did conflict strengthen and weaken the relationships between city-states?
  A. Have students each read the "Weekly Connection" article and discuss the prompt with a classmate before beginning to write.

sections.name: Teacher Background Knowledge
sections.content:
Ancient Athens is known for its invention of democracy, which emerged after reformers challenged the rule of wealthy nobles. Cleisthenes established a council and an Assembly, giving ordinary citizens a voice. Athenian democracy flourished under Pericles, who furthered citizen participation by introducing pay for public service. Despite Athens's achievements during the golden age, Plato criticized democracy, advocating for philosopher-kings. Nevertheless, Athenian democracy's influence endures, with its principles inspiring modern democratic systems worldwide.
The Greco-Persian Wars were fought from about 492 B.C.E. to 449 B.C.E. between the small city-states of Greece and the First Persian Empire, which was one of the largest empires of all time. The wars ended with the Greeks maintaining their independence from Persia. Greeks remember the two most famous battles of the Persian Wars as evidence of their people's great courage and resourcefulness. At the Battle of Marathon in 490 B.C.E., an army from the city of Athens expelled the Persians from the Greek mainland. At the Battle of Salamis in 480 B.C.E., the superior Greek navy lured Persian ships into a strait, trapping them and ultimately destroying the Persian navy. This battle ended any hope of Persian forces taking mainland Greece.
From 460 to 446 B.C.E. and then 431 to 404 B.C.E., Athens and Sparta fought against each other in what is known as the Peloponnesian Wars. These two rival city-states fought for dominance of the southern part of the Greek peninsula, which is known as the Peloponnese. Sparta won the war, but the long conflict weakened both cities. Other parts of Greece soon came to dominate the region. First Thebes and then Macedon rose to power.

The Greek world split into two hostile camps, and this deep division led to the Peloponnesian War. The conflict unfolded in two long stages and drew in almost all the major city-states. Athens at first controlled the seas, but overcrowding inside its walls and a deadly plague weakened the city. Sparta later received Persian money to build a stronger navy, destroyed much of the Athenian fleet, and forced Athens to surrender. For a time, harsh pro-Spartan rulers known as the Thirty Tyrants took power in Athens. After years of fighting, many Greek city-states were left weakened and divided.
This weakness opened the way for a northern kingdom, Macedon, to dominate Greece. King Philip of Macedon built a new kind of army that trained farmers and commoners to fight in a tight phalanx formation with long spears, supported by a strong cavalry. At the Battle of Chaeronea in 338 B.C.E., Philip and his son Alexander defeated a united force from Athens and Thebes and drew most Greek city-states into a league led by Macedon. Shortly afterward, Philip was assassinated, and Alexander became king.
Alexander set out to conquer the Persian Empire and succeeded, then went on to conquer Egypt and push his armies as far as India. His troops finally refused to march farther, and he turned back, but Greek influence had already spread across a vast region. Wherever Alexander went, his armies and settlers carried Greek language, stories, art, and building styles. Local rulers and communities began to adopt Greek architecture, build theaters and marketplaces, and use Greek in government and trade. Alexander also showed interest in the cultures he encountered, studying astronomy in Mesopotamia, exploring the Nile, and collecting objects to display in museums. At the same time, he could be brutally harsh, as when he destroyed Thebes after a rebellion.
Alexander died at age 32 without a clear heir, and his generals struggled for control. After long conflicts known as the Successor Wars, his empire broke into several Hellenistic kingdoms ruled by leaders, such as Antigonus, Seleucus, and Ptolemy. They governed lands that stretched from Macedonia and the Greek city-states to Egypt, Syria, Mesopotamia, Persia, and Central Asia. Greek became the language of many royal courts and official documents. New or expanded cities such as Alexandria, Antioch, Pergamon, and Ai Khanum combined Greek plans and buildings with local traditions. The Museum of Alexandria and the Library of Alexandria gathered texts from many cultures and became centers of study. Inscriptions such as the Rosetta Stone, written in Egyptian and Greek, and translations of older writings into Greek show how widely Hellenistic culture reached.
Over time, the Hellenistic kingdoms weakened and eventually came under Roman control. Greek influence did not disappear. Instead, Roman language, art, architecture, and political ideas absorbed and reshaped Greek models.

sections.name: Teacher Background Knowledge
sections.content:
Ancient Athens is known for its invention of democracy, which emerged after reformers challenged the rule of wealthy nobles. Cleisthenes established a council and an Assembly, giving ordinary citizens a voice. Athenian democracy flourished under Pericles, who furthered citizen participation by introducing pay for public service. Despite Athens's achievements during the golden age, Plato criticized democracy, advocating for philosopher-kings. Nevertheless, Athenian democracy's influence endures, with its principles inspiring modern democratic systems worldwide.
The Greco-Persian Wars were fought from about 492 B.C.E. to 449 B.C.E. between the small city-states of Greece and the First Persian Empire, which was one of the largest empires of all time. The wars ended with the Greeks maintaining their independence from Persia. Greeks remember the two most famous battles of the Persian Wars as evidence of their people's great courage and resourcefulness. At the Battle of Marathon in 490 B.C.E., an army from the city of Athens expelled the Persians from the Greek mainland. At the Battle of Salamis in 480 B.C.E., the superior Greek navy lured Persian ships into a strait, trapping them and ultimately destroying the Persian navy. This battle ended any hope of Persian forces taking mainland Greece.
From 460 to 446 B.C.E. and then 431 to 404 B.C.E., Athens and Sparta fought against each other in what is known as the Peloponnesian Wars. These two rival city-states fought for dominance of the southern part of the Greek peninsula, which is known as the Peloponnese. Sparta won the war, but the long conflict weakened both cities. Other parts of Greece soon came to dominate the region. First Thebes and then Macedon rose to power.

sections.name: Unit Summary
sections.content:
Ancient Greece began on a small, mountainous peninsula that stretched into the Mediterranean Sea between Europe and Asia. Steep mountains, scattered valleys, and many islands made travel difficult and kept communities apart. Villages and towns developed independently and grew into city-states, or poleis, each with its own government, laws, and identity. People thought of themselves first as citizens of places such as Athens, Sparta, or Thebes, not simply as "Greek." The sea was central to life. Limited good farmland pushed many people toward shipbuilding, sailing, fishing, and trade, so harbors and coastlines became busy centers of exchange.
Over time, different city-states experienced different types of government. Some were monarchies ruled by kings. Others became oligarchies where a small group of wealthy men shared power. At times, individuals seized control as tyrants. In Athens, reformers eventually pushed the city toward democracy. Cleisthenes reorganized Athenian society in 508 B.C.E., creating a council of 500 citizens chosen by lottery and an assembly where citizens debated and voted on laws. Later, Pericles strengthened this system by paying jurors and officials so poorer citizens could serve. Under his leadership, Athens entered a golden age of art, architecture, and philosophy. At the same time, thinkers such as Plato criticized democracy and argued that only wise, trained philosopher-kings should rule. Even with its limits and critics, Athenian democracy introduced the lasting idea that citizens should have a voice in how they are governed.
Religion shaped everyday life and public spaces. The Greeks practiced a polytheistic religion that honored many gods and goddesses who looked and acted like humans. Zeus ruled the sky with thunderbolts, Poseidon ruled the sea, and Athena represented wisdom and war. These and other deities were believed to live on Mount Olympus, yet they often stepped into human affairs. Myths told stories of the gods falling in love, arguing, taking sides in wars, and seeking revenge, which helped people understand the divine through familiar emotions. Families kept small household altars where they offered food, wine, and incense to the gods. Cities built great temples and sanctuaries, such as the Acropolis in Athens and the sanctuary of Apollo at Delphi. Festivals with processions, music, athletics, and drama, including the Panathenaia and the Olympic Games, brought communities together and reinforced a shared Greek identity. Greek religion had no single holy book; instead, myths and rituals taught moral lessons and linked worship to daily life.
Ideas also spread through philosophy and art. Socrates encouraged people to question their beliefs. Plato wrote about justice, government, and truth, and founded the Academy in Athens. Aristotle focused on observation and logic, studied science and politics, and founded the Lyceum. Their work influenced education, civic life, and ways of thinking about knowledge. Greek architecture, especially the use of columns and carefully planned temples, expressed values of order, balance, and beauty. Buildings on the Acropolis, including the Parthenon, showed both religious devotion and civic pride.
Greek city-states sometimes united against outside threats. The Persian Empire grew into one of the largest empires in history and controlled Greek cities in Ionia. When these cities rebelled and Athens helped them, the Persian kings tried to punish mainland Greece. Athens and Sparta formed an alliance, combining Athenian naval strength with Spartan land forces. In battles such as Marathon, Salamis, and Plataea, Greek strategies and the use of local geography helped smaller Greek forces defeat larger Persian armies. These victories protected Greek independence and encouraged many people to see themselves as part of a larger Greek world. After the wars, Athens led a naval alliance called the Delian League. Member city-states sent ships or money to a shared treasury, which helped Athens grow richer and more powerful and contributed to its golden age.
As Persian threats faded, fear and rivalry grew within Greece. Athens began to treat Delian League members less like partners and more like subjects, forcing cities such as Thasos to remain in the alliance and continue paying tribute. Sparta and allies such as Corinth formed the Peloponnesian League, claiming to defend the independence of city-states that did not want Athenian control. The Greek world split into two hostile camps, and this deep division led to the Peloponnesian War. The conflict unfolded in two long stages and drew in almost all the major city-states. Athens at first controlled the seas, but overcrowding inside its walls and a deadly plague weakened the city. Sparta later received Persian money to build a stronger navy, destroyed much of the Athenian fleet, and forced Athens to surrender. For a time, harsh pro-Spartan rulers known as the Thirty Tyrants took power in Athens. After years of fighting, many Greek city-states were left weakened and divided.
This weakness opened the way for a northern kingdom, Macedon, to dominate Greece. King Philip of Macedon built a new kind of army that trained farmers and commoners to fight in a tight phalanx formation with long spears, supported by a strong cavalry. At the Battle of Chaeronea in 338 B.C.E., Philip and his son Alexander defeated a united force from Athens and Thebes and drew most Greek city-states into a league led by Macedon. Shortly afterward, Philip was assassinated, and Alexander became king.
Alexander set out to conquer the Persian Empire and succeeded, then went on to conquer Egypt and push his armies as far as India. His troops finally refused to march farther, and he turned back, but Greek influence had already spread across a vast region. Wherever Alexander went, his armies and settlers carried Greek language, stories, art, and building styles. Local rulers and communities began to adopt Greek architecture, build theaters and marketplaces, and use Greek in government and trade. Alexander also showed interest in the cultures he encountered, studying astronomy in Mesopotamia, exploring the Nile, and collecting objects to display in museums. At the same time, he could be brutally harsh, as when he destroyed Thebes after a rebellion.
Alexander died at age 32 without a clear heir, and his generals struggled for control. After long conflicts known as the Successor Wars, his empire broke into several Hellenistic kingdoms ruled by leaders, such as Antigonus, Seleucus, and Ptolemy. They governed lands that stretched from Macedonia and the Greek city-states to Egypt, Syria, Mesopotamia, Persia, and Central Asia. Greek became the language of many royal courts and official documents. New or expanded cities such as Alexandria, Antioch, Pergamon, and Ai Khanum combined Greek plans and buildings with local traditions. The Museum of Alexandria and the Library of Alexandria gathered texts from many cultures and became centers of study. Inscriptions such as the Rosetta Stone, written in Egyptian and Greek, and translations of older writings into Greek show how widely Hellenistic culture reached.
Over time, the Hellenistic kingdoms weakened and eventually came under Roman control. Greek influence did not disappear. Instead, Roman language, art, architecture, and political ideas absorbed and reshaped Greek models.

sections.name: Teacher Background Knowledge
sections.content:
Ancient Athens is known for its invention of democracy, which emerged after reformers challenged the rule of wealthy nobles. Cleisthenes established a council and an Assembly, giving ordinary citizens a voice. Athenian democracy flourished under Pericles, who furthered citizen participation by introducing pay for public service. Despite Athens's achievements during the golden age, Plato criticized democracy, advocating for philosopher-kings. Nevertheless, Athenian democracy's influence endures, with its principles inspiring modern democratic systems worldwide.
The Greco-Persian Wars were fought from about 492 B.C.E. to 449 B.C.E. between the small city-states of Greece and the First Persian Empire, which was one of the largest empires of all time. The wars ended with the Greeks maintaining their independence from Persia. Greeks remember the two most famous battles of the Persian Wars as evidence of their people's great courage and resourcefulness. At the Battle of Marathon in 490 B.C.E., an army from the city of Athens expelled the Persians from the Greek mainland. At the Battle of Salamis in 480 B.C.E., the superior Greek navy lured Persian ships into a strait, trapping them and ultimately destroying the Persian navy. This battle ended any hope of Persian forces taking mainland Greece.
From 460 to 446 B.C.E. and then 431 to 404 B.C.E., Athens and Sparta fought against each other in what is known as the Peloponnesian Wars. These two rival city-states fought for dominance of the southern part of the Greek peninsula, which is known as the Peloponnese. Sparta won the war, but the long conflict weakened both cities. Other parts of Greece soon came to dominate the region. First Thebes and then Macedon rose to power.

sections.content:
Students will be able to analyze the causes and consequences of the Persian and Peloponnesian Wars.
Students will be able to explain how Athens and Sparta sometimes cooperated to defend Greece and other times fought each other for power.
Students will be able to evaluate how these wars shaped the strength and eventual decline of the Greek city-states.

sections.name: Unit Summary
sections.content:
Ancient Greece began on a small, mountainous peninsula that stretched into the Mediterranean Sea between Europe and Asia. Steep mountains, scattered valleys, and many islands made travel difficult and kept communities apart. Villages and towns developed independently and grew into city-states, or poleis, each with its own government, laws, and identity. People thought of themselves first as citizens of places such as Athens, Sparta, or Thebes, not simply as "Greek." The sea was central to life. Limited good farmland pushed many people toward shipbuilding, sailing, fishing, and trade, so harbors and coastlines became busy centers of exchange.
Over time, different city-states experienced different types of government. Some were monarchies ruled by kings. Others became oligarchies where a small group of wealthy men shared power. At times, individuals seized control as tyrants. In Athens, reformers eventually pushed the city toward democracy. Cleisthenes reorganized Athenian society in 508 B.C.E., creating a council of 500 citizens chosen by lottery and an assembly where citizens debated and voted on laws. Later, Pericles strengthened this system by paying jurors and officials so poorer citizens could serve. Under his leadership, Athens entered a golden age of art, architecture, and philosophy. At the same time, thinkers such as Plato criticized democracy and argued that only wise, trained philosopher-kings should rule. Even with its limits and critics, Athenian democracy introduced the lasting idea that citizens should have a voice in how they are governed.
Religion shaped everyday life and public spaces. The Greeks practiced a polytheistic religion that honored many gods and goddesses who looked and acted like humans. Zeus ruled the sky with thunderbolts, Poseidon ruled the sea, and Athena represented wisdom and war. These and other deities were believed to live on Mount Olympus, yet they often stepped into human affairs. Myths told stories of the gods falling in love, arguing, taking sides in wars, and seeking revenge, which helped people understand the divine through familiar emotions. Families kept small household altars where they offered food, wine, and incense to the gods. Cities built great temples and sanctuaries, such as the Acropolis in Athens and the sanctuary of Apollo at Delphi. Festivals with processions, music, athletics, and drama, including the Panathenaia and the Olympic Games, brought communities together and reinforced a shared Greek identity. Greek religion had no single holy book; instead, myths and rituals taught moral lessons and linked worship to daily life.
Ideas also spread through philosophy and art. Socrates encouraged people to question their beliefs. Plato wrote about justice, government, and truth, and founded the Academy in Athens. Aristotle focused on observation and logic, studied science and politics, and founded the Lyceum. Their work influenced education, civic life, and ways of thinking about knowledge. Greek architecture, especially the use of columns and carefully planned temples, expressed values of order, balance, and beauty. Buildings on the Acropolis, including the Parthenon, showed both religious devotion and civic pride.
Greek city-states sometimes united against outside threats. The Persian Empire grew into one of the largest empires in history and controlled Greek cities in Ionia. When these cities rebelled and Athens helped them, the Persian kings tried to punish mainland Greece. Athens and Sparta formed an alliance, combining Athenian naval strength with Spartan land forces. In battles such as Marathon, Salamis, and Plataea, Greek strategies and the use of local geography helped smaller Greek forces defeat larger Persian armies. These victories protected Greek independence and encouraged many people to see themselves as part of a larger Greek world. After the wars, Athens led a naval alliance called the Delian League. Member city-states sent ships or money to a shared treasury, which helped Athens grow richer and more powerful and contributed to its golden age.
As Persian threats faded, fear and rivalry grew within Greece. Athens began to treat Delian League members less like partners and more like subjects, forcing cities such as Thasos to remain in the alliance and continue paying tribute. Sparta and allies such as Corinth formed the Peloponnesian League, claiming to defend the independence of city-states that did not want Athenian control. The Greek world split into two hostile camps, and this deep division led to the Peloponnesian War. The conflict unfolded in two long stages and drew in almost all the major city-states. Athens at first controlled the seas, but overcrowding inside its walls and a deadly plague weakened the city. Sparta later received Persian money to build a stronger navy, destroyed much of the Athenian fleet, and forced Athens to surrender. For a time, harsh pro-Spartan rulers known as the Thirty Tyrants took power in Athens. After years of fighting, many Greek city-states were left weakened and divided.
This weakness opened the way for a northern kingdom, Macedon, to dominate Greece. King Philip of Macedon built a new kind of army that trained farmers and commoners to fight in a tight phalanx formation with long spears, supported by a strong cavalry. At the Battle of Chaeronea in 338 B.C.E., Philip and his son Alexander defeated a united force from Athens and Thebes and drew most Greek city-states into a league led by Macedon. Shortly afterward, Philip was assassinated, and Alexander became king.
Alexander set out to conquer the Persian Empire and succeeded, then went on to conquer Egypt and push his armies as far as India. His troops finally refused to march farther, and he turned back, but Greek influence had already spread across a vast region. Wherever Alexander went, his armies and settlers carried Greek language, stories, art, and building styles. Local rulers and communities began to adopt Greek architecture, build theaters and marketplaces, and use Greek in government and trade. Alexander also showed interest in the cultures he encountered, studying astronomy in Mesopotamia, exploring the Nile, and collecting objects to display in museums. At the same time, he could be brutally harsh, as when he destroyed Thebes after a rebellion.
Alexander died at age 32 without a clear heir, and his generals struggled for control. After long conflicts known as the Successor Wars, his empire broke into several Hellenistic kingdoms ruled by leaders, such as Antigonus, Seleucus, and Ptolemy. They governed lands that stretched from Macedonia and the Greek city-states to Egypt, Syria, Mesopotamia, Persia, and Central Asia. Greek became the language of many royal courts and official documents. New or expanded cities such as Alexandria, Antioch, Pergamon, and Ai Khanum combined Greek plans and buildings with local traditions. The Museum of Alexandria and the Library of Alexandria gathered texts from many cultures and became centers of study. Inscriptions such as the Rosetta Stone, written in Egyptian and Greek, and translations of older writings into Greek show how widely Hellenistic culture reached.
Over time, the Hellenistic kingdoms weakened and eventually came under Roman control. Greek influence did not disappear. Instead, Roman language, art, architecture, and political ideas absorbed and reshaped Greek models.

sections.name: Let’s Write
sections.content:
In what important ways were Athens and Sparta different from each other, and how did those differences shape the history of Greece?
How did the ideas of philosophers such as Socrates, Plato, and Aristotle challenge people in ancient Greece to think differently about government?
How did the Persian Wars and the Peloponnesian War each change the balance of power among Greek city-states and affect the future of Greece?

sections.name: Apply What You Learned
sections.content:
Choose one of the activities to show what you've learned:
WRITE:
Which city-state in ancient Greece do you think you would have wanted to live in, such as Athens, Sparta, or another polis? Write one or two paragraphs explaining your choice and the reasons for your choice, using facts you have learned.
Was Alexander the Great truly "great"? Write 1-2 paragraphs explaining your opinion using examples from his conquests and how he treated the places he ruled.
Which physical feature of Greece, such as mountains, islands, or seas, do you think had the biggest effect on how people lived? Write 1-2 paragraphs explaining your choice, and use specific examples to show how that feature affected travel, trade, or farming.
RESEARCH/PRESENT:
Choose one Greek god, goddess, or hero you would like to learn more about. Create a poster or a presentation explaining what Greek people believed about this deity and how the people worshipped the deity.
Research a famous Greek building, such as the Parthenon, or a temple from another city-state. Find out what it was used for and what its main features were. Create a labeled drawing or model and present at least three facts about the building.
SPEAK:
Work in pairs to discuss what you think was the most important idea the Greeks contributed to the world (for example, democracy, drama, philosophy, or architecture). Explain your choice, and listen to what your partner thinks. Use reasoning and examples to defend your view.
Choose a Greek myth you have learned about. Work in pairs and retell the myth in your own words, making sure to explain what lesson or message the myth teaches. Then listen to your partner's myth and ask questions about it.
Write and deliver a speech from the perspective of Pericles, urging the citizens of Athens to take pride in their democratic government. Your speech should accurately describe the features and rules of Athenian democracy.
PLAN:
Work in pairs to design a mini Greek festival for your class. Plan activities taken from ancient Greek culture, including at least one activity involving food, one involving games or sports, and one involving arts or theater. Make a list of what you would need and how you would decorate.
Work in a small group to plan a short play based on a Greek myth or historical event, such as the Battle of Marathon or life in Athens. Decide on characters, scenes, and simple props. Write a brief outline of what each person will do.
Work with a partner to design a student government based loosely on Athenian democracy. Decide who can vote, how leaders are chosen, and how decisions will be made. Write down your rules and be ready to explain why you chose them.
DRAW:
Draw a map of ancient Greece showing the surrounding seas, mountains, and at least three important city-states. Label each part and add a key. On another piece of paper, write a paragraph explaining how geography might affect those city-states
Draw a scene from daily life in either Athens or Sparta. Include buildings, clothing, and activities that match what you have learned. Be ready to explain what each part of your picture shows.
PODCAST: Choose a podcast from this unit. Listen to the podcast individually or in pairs.
Draw a picture to represent what you learned.
Write a list of things you learned.
Create 3-5 questions from the podcast.
Create a graphic organizer from the podcast content.
